comprProduits.hero_subtitle: What your bank offers you — and what it doesn't always tell you.
comprProduits.intro: Banking products are not all bad. Some fulfill a useful function. But understanding exactly how they work — their real fees, their taxation, their constraints — is essential for making an informed decision.
comprProduits.s1_p1: The Belgian savings account works with two components: a base rate (paid from the first day) and a fidelity premium (acquired only if the capital remains on the account for 12 consecutive months).
comprProduits.s1_p2: The fidelity premium is a behavioural mechanism: it penalises unforeseen withdrawals and secures low-cost financing for the bank. If you withdraw before 12 months, you lose the premium on the amount withdrawn.
comprProduits.s1_fiscal_text: The first €1,020 of annual interest per person (amount for 2026 income, indexed annually) is exempt from withholding tax. Beyond that, each additional euro of interest is taxed at 15% — and not 30% like other mobile income: this is one of the rare tax advantages of the regulated savings account.
comprProduits.s1_verdict_p1: The savings account has a precise and legitimate function: your emergency fund (3 to 6 months of expenses), available at any time without risk of capital loss.
comprProduits.s1_verdict_p2: For this function, it is irreplaceable. For everything else — medium or long-term savings — every year spent in a savings account is a year where your purchasing power stagnates or retreats against inflation.
comprProduits.s2_title: The savings bond and term account
comprProduits.s2_p1: These products lock your capital for a fixed duration (1 to 10 years) in exchange for a guaranteed rate. The certainty of the return is paid for in two ways:
comprProduits.s2_pt1_body: Unlike the savings account, all interest is subject to 30% withholding tax from the first cent. A displayed gross rate of 3.10% becomes 2.17% net.
comprProduits.s2_pt2_body: Early withdrawal is generally impossible or penalised. Your capital is locked until the contractual maturity.
comprProduits.s2_verdict_p1: In almost all cases, a savings bond or term account is inferior to a simpler alternative.
comprProduits.s2_verdict_p2: After the 30% withholding tax, a 3.10% gross rate becomes 2.17% net — all while locking your capital. A money market ETF like XEON offers a return close to the ECB policy rate — generally higher than the best term accounts after tax, while remaining totally liquid and without triggering annual withholding tax.
comprProduits.s2_verdict_p3: The only argument for a savings bond would be not wanting to open an account with a broker. In this case, a high-rate savings account (vdk Rythme, Belfius Flow) remains more flexible for the same risk profile.
comprProduits.s3_title_sub: (savings-insurance, not a banking product in the strict sense)
comprProduits.s3_p1: Branch 21 is technically a life insurance contract offered by insurance companies, not a bank deposit — even if banks commonly distribute it. It offers a guaranteed interest rate, supplemented by a possible non-guaranteed profit sharing. Its taxation is specific:
comprProduits.s3_bullet1: A 2% premium tax is levied on each payment before any investment
comprProduits.s3_bullet2: If you redeem before 8 years, a 30% withholding tax applies to interest — except in case of the insured's death (130% clause), which exempts from withholding regardless of the duration
comprProduits.s3_bullet3: After 8 years, interest is exempt from withholding tax
comprProduits.s3_warning: The 8-year rule trap: a product presented as 'secure' actually locks your capital for 8 years to be tax-optimal. And the 2% tax on each premium immediately reduces your invested capital.
comprProduits.s3_example_text: Out of €10,000 paid, only €9,800 is invested (after 2% tax). At a guaranteed rate of 2.50%, it takes about a year just to recover this initial tax.
comprProduits.s3_verdict_p1: Branch 21 is structurally disadvantageous for the ordinary investor:
comprProduits.s3_b1: 2% premium tax deducted immediately from each payment
comprProduits.s3_b2: Capital locked for 8 years to avoid 30% withholding
comprProduits.s3_b3: Guaranteed rate of 2.50% to 3.10% — barely higher than the best savings accounts, without the liquidity
comprProduits.s3_verdict_p2: On a short horizon (<8 years), XEON is more profitable and liquid. On a long horizon (>8 years), an equity ETF structurally surpasses this guaranteed return.
comprProduits.s3_verdict_p3: Branch 21 wins on no horizon for an investor who accepts using a broker. Note: within the specific framework of long-term savings (see next section), Branch 21 and 23 products may benefit from a 30% tax reduction and an exemption from the 2026 CGT — which can reduce the disadvantage in very specific cases, without changing the general conclusion.
comprProduits.s_lt_title: Long-term savings — the overlooked tax scheme
comprProduits.s_lt_p1: Long-term savings is a distinct federal tax scheme, separate from pension savings, though the two are often confused. It allows the premiums paid into a life insurance contract (Branch 21 or Branch 23) to be tax-deducted at a rate of 30%, with a contribution ceiling significantly higher than pension savings.
comprProduits.s_lt_ceil_text: Up to €2,450/year (depends on net taxable professional income) — Maximum tax reduction: €735/year (30% × €2,450) — Practical rule: a full-time employee can generally deduct ~€75/month. For a gross salary ≥ €45,000/year, the maximum ceiling is accessible.
comprProduits.s_lt_stackable: This scheme is stackable with pension savings: both can be used simultaneously, for a potential combined tax benefit of over €1,000/year.
comprProduits.s_lt_mortgage_title: The mortgage conflict — a complex and often misunderstood rule
comprProduits.s_lt_mortgage_p1: For old loans (pre-2016 in Flanders and Wallonia, pre-2017 in Brussels): long-term savings and mortgage deductions share the same 'federal tax basket' capped at €2,450. In practice, capital repayments and outstanding balance insurance premiums typically fill this basket entirely — leaving no room for long-term savings.
comprProduits.s_lt_mortgage_p2: For recent loans (post-2016 in Flanders, post-2017 in Brussels, post-2025 in Wallonia): mortgage tax benefits now fall under a separate regional system. The federal basket is entirely free, allowing you to fully benefit from long-term savings on top of your mortgage deduction.
comprProduits.s_lt_mortgage_p3: Simple rule: if your loan was taken out after 2016 (Flanders/Wallonia) or 2017 (Brussels), you can most likely combine both benefits. If in doubt, check with your local tax office.
comprProduits.s_lt_pt1_body: Levied on each payment before any investment — identical to any Branch 21 or 23 product.
comprProduits.s_lt_pt2_body: At age 60, the state levies 10% on the total accumulated capital and return. If you subscribed after age 55, the tax is due after 10 years of contract. Payments made after age 60 are no longer subject to this tax.
comprProduits.s_lt_pt3_title: Exemption from the 2026 capital gains tax
comprProduits.s_lt_pt3_body: Life insurance contracts qualifying for the long-term savings tax reduction are permanently exempt from the new 2026 capital gains tax (CGT). Important: this exemption is separate from the 10% exit tax — both coexist. The exit tax is due at 60; the 2026 CGT is not.
comprProduits.s_lt_math_label: The maths are more nuanced than they appear
comprProduits.s_lt_math_p1: Over 30 years with a net annual payment of €1,715/year (= €2,450 minus the €735 tax refund), the combination of tax benefit + CGT exemption can, in the best cases, produce a result comparable to a World ETF — or even slightly higher. But this requires all of the following conditions to be met simultaneously:
comprProduits.s_lt_math_c1: Access to the federal tax basket (recent loan or no loan)
comprProduits.s_lt_math_p2: Beyond ~1.5–1.8%/year in total annual fees, the ETF systematically regains the advantage — even with the full tax benefit. Most products distributed through traditional banking networks far exceed this threshold.
comprProduits.s_lt_verdict_p1: Long-term savings is a real, overlooked scheme that is potentially relevant for taxpayers with access to the federal basket. But the verdict depends entirely on the product chosen.
comprProduits.s_lt_verdict_p2: With a low-cost product (~1.4%/year), the net return can be competitive with an ETF. With a standard banking product (1.7–2.5%/year in fees), the ETF wins structurally — the tax advantage does not offset the fee gap over 30 years.
comprProduits.s_lt_verdict_p3: The 10% exit tax at 60 and the lock-up of capital until that age remain significant structural constraints, regardless of fees.
comprProduits.s4_title: Pension savings — the myth of the tax advantage
comprProduits.s4_p1: Pension savings appeal through their immediate tax advantage, available in two options:
comprProduits.s4_opt1_text: Up to €1,050/year paid → 30% tax reduction → up to €315 recovered.
comprProduits.s4_opt2_text: Up to €1,350/year paid → 25% tax reduction → up to €337 recovered.
comprProduits.s4_threshold: Tipping point: the increased option only becomes advantageous from €1,260/year in payments. Below this, the standard option is preferable.
comprProduits.s4_p2: But three elements temper this enthusiasm:
comprProduits.s4_pt1_body: Belgian pension savings funds show TERs of 1.25% to 1.50%, to which are added entry fees of 0% to 3% depending on the institution. Over 30 years, these fees can erase a significant part of the initial tax advantage.
comprProduits.s4_pt2_title: The exit tax — on a fictitious capital
comprProduits.s4_pt2_body: At age 60, the state levies an anticipatory tax of 8% — not on your real capital, but on a fictitious capital calculated as if your fund (branch 23) had earned 4.75% per year from the start. This fictitious calculation applies to branch 23 pension savings funds only; branch 21 follows a different mechanism. If your fund underperformed the 4.75% threshold, your effective tax rate on the real return increases considerably.
comprProduits.s4_pt3_body: Capital is locked until age 60. Early withdrawal triggers taxation at the personal income tax (PIT) rate of 33%, to which are added municipal surcharges — this is not a withholding tax, but a final tax that can exceed 35% depending on the municipality of residence.
comprProduits.s4_verdict_p1: The 30% tax advantage seems considerable — but it is a one-time bonus, paid once a year, that does not compound.
comprProduits.s4_verdict_p2: Meanwhile, the performance gap between the pension fund (~4%) and an ETF (~8%) compounds every year for decades.
comprProduits.s4_verdict_p3: Our simulator demonstrates it concretely: over 30 years at €87.50/month (tax ceiling), an All-in-one ETF surpasses Argenta pension savings from the 11th year — and ends with €33,000 more at 30 years, despite the best fund on the market (0% entry fees) and the maximum tax advantage.
comprProduits.s4_verdict_p4: Added to this: capital locked until 60, an exit tax of 8%, and annual management fees of 1.25% to 1.50%.
comprProduits.s4_verdict_p5: Our conclusion: pension savings are not an investment strategy — they are a tax product that structurally underperforms over the long term. Keep your capital free and invest it in an ETF.
comprProduits.s5_title: Active funds — why they cost so much
comprProduits.s5_p1: Active mutual funds are the most lucrative product for banks — and often the least advantageous for the investor.
comprProduits.s5_p2: Their economic model relies on retrocessions: the management company levies a TER of 1.50% to 2.50%, part of which is paid back to the distributing bank to remunerate its commercial effort. MiFID II (2018) imposed more transparency — banks must now publish a KID/DICI document mentioning total costs — and banned retrocessions for independent advisors. But traditional banking networks, which operate as tied distributors, can still receive these commissions. In practice, the network client remains exposed to a structural selection bias: recommended funds are those that generate the most retrocessions, not necessarily those that offer the best net return for the investor.
comprProduits.s5_quote: Over 20 years, between 85% and 92% of active funds underperform their benchmark index after fees. This is not a question of incompetence — it is a mathematical reality linked to the cost of active management.
comprProduits.s5_verdict_p1: There is no general case where a bank active fund is the best choice for a long-term investor.
comprProduits.s5_verdict_p2: Between 85% and 92% of active funds underperform their benchmark index over 20 years, after fees. This is not an opinion — it is the documented result of decades of academic studies and Morningstar data.
comprProduits.s5_verdict_p3: An ETF with 0.17% TER versus an active fund at 1.73%: the difference seems small. Over 30 years, it often represents more than 30% of the final capital.

immobilierVsEtf.hero_subtitle: The honest comparison that nobody will give you at your bank.
immobilierVsEtf.intro_p1: Belgium has a special relationship with bricks. « Een steen in de maag » — a brick in the stomach — the expression alone summarizes a deep cultural conviction: bricks and mortar are safe, concrete, and real.
immobilierVsEtf.intro_p2: But is it really the best investment for you? The honest answer is: it depends. Not on the markets, not on interest rates — but on what you are willing to put in besides your money.
immobilierVsEtf.s1_p1: With equal capital and no loan, Belgian residential real estate has historically delivered a total return of 6% to 8% per year: 3% to 4% property value appreciation, plus 3% to 4% net rental yield (after charges, vacancies, and maintenance).
immobilierVsEtf.s1_p2: A global ETF like IMIE has historically delivered 7% to 9% per year over the long term.
immobilierVsEtf.s1_p3: The observation: with identical capital and no leverage, both strategies offer comparable returns. The fundamental difference lies elsewhere — in concentration risk, liquidity, and the time you dedicate to it.
immobilierVsEtf.s2_title: The superpower of real estate: leverage
immobilierVsEtf.s2_p1: This is the strongest argument in favor of real estate — and the only one that ETFs cannot easily replicate.
immobilierVsEtf.s2_p2: A bank will never lend you €200,000 to buy ETFs. It will do so for bricks.
immobilierVsEtf.s2_example_p1: You buy a €250,000 apartment with a €50,000 deposit. You receive 100% of the rent and capital gain on a €250,000 property, while only having mobilized €50,000. A 6% return on €250,000 represents €15,000/year — which is a 30% return on your initial €50,000 stake.
immobilierVsEtf.s2_p3: This is where real estate mathematically beats ETFs: not in the property's yield, but in the amplification by bank leverage.
immobilierVsEtf.s2_p4: The flip side: leverage amplifies gains in both directions. An apartment that loses 20% in value with an 80% loan wipes out your entire deposit.
immobilierVsEtf.s3_title: Structural advantages of Belgian real estate
immobilierVsEtf.s3_intro: Belgium offers a tax framework that has historically been favorable to private landlords:
immobilierVsEtf.s3_item1_title: Taxation on cadastral income, not real rents:
immobilierVsEtf.s3_item1_p: If you rent to an individual for private use, you are not taxed on the actual rents received, but on the indexed Cadastral Income (CI) increased by 40% — often much lower than the economic reality.
immobilierVsEtf.s3_item2_p: In Belgium, the capital gain on the resale of a property held for more than 5 years is not taxed (in normal management). This is a considerable advantage compared to many neighboring countries.
immobilierVsEtf.s3_item3_p: Belgian leases allow for annual indexation of rents based on the health index. The value of bricks and mortar structurally follows inflation over the long term.
immobilierVsEtf.s3_item4_p: A property cannot be sold on impulse in 30 seconds. This illiquidity — often presented as a flaw — paradoxically protects the investor against their own reactions during crises.
immobilierVsEtf.s4_title: What real estate really costs you
immobilierVsEtf.s4_intro: The frictions associated with real estate are massive and often underestimated.
immobilierVsEtf.s4_item1_p1: In Belgium, for an investment property (excluding own residence), registration duties amount to 12% in Flanders, 12.5% in Brussels and Wallonia. With notary fees and mortgage deed costs, you lose about 15% of the property value on the day of purchase. On €250,000, that's €37,500 gone before the property even generates a single euro.
immobilierVsEtf.s4_item1_p2: With an ETF: 0.12% TOB. On €250,000, that's €300.
immobilierVsEtf.s4_item2_p: If you need €10,000 urgently, you cannot sell the toilet in your apartment. With an ETF, you sell exactly what you need, and the money is available in 48 hours.
immobilierVsEtf.s4_item3_p: Rental real estate is not a passive income. Managing tenants, vacancies, water leaks at 10 PM, general meetings of co-owners, energy standards compliance (mandatory PEB), building manager. It's a second part-time job. An ETF requires 5 minutes a month.
immobilierVsEtf.s4_item4_p: Annual property tax, fire insurance, non-recoverable co-ownership charges, provisions for major works — these costs silently eat away at the gross return.
immobilierVsEtf.s4_warning: The gross rental yield displayed by real estate agencies systematically omits these fees. A 5% gross yield can become 2% to 3% net once all real costs are taken into account.
immobilierVsEtf.s5_p1: These are two different strategies aimed at different profiles — not direct competitors.
immobilierVsEtf.s5_immo_title: Real estate is for you if:
immobilierVsEtf.s5_immo_li1: You want to use your borrowing capacity to amplify your returns via leverage
immobilierVsEtf.s5_immo_li2: You are willing to dedicate time and energy to it (selection, management, maintenance)
immobilierVsEtf.s5_immo_li3: You already have a solid passive investment base and are looking to diversify
immobilierVsEtf.s5_etf_li1: You want to invest without dedicating your free time to it
immobilierVsEtf.s5_etf_li2: You are a beginner and do not yet have a €50,000 deposit
immobilierVsEtf.s5_etf_li4: You want immediate global diversification with any amount
immobilierVsEtf.s5_p2: The real question is not « real estate or ETF? » — it's « do I have the capital, the time, and the appetite to manage a property? »
immobilierVsEtf.s5_p3: If the answer is yes to all three: real estate with leverage can be an excellent complementary strategy. If not: ETFs do the job without friction, without stress, and with comparable returns.
immobilierVsEtf.comp_left_levier: Leverage possible via credit (amplifier depending on yield/rate spread)
immobilierVsEtf.s2b_title: The Interest Rate — the Variable That Changes Everything
immobilierVsEtf.s2b_p1: Leverage is only beneficial if your net return exceeds your borrowing cost. This is known as the positive leverage effect.
immobilierVsEtf.s2b_rule_pos: If net return on property > mortgage rate → leverage amplifies your gains ✅
immobilierVsEtf.s2b_rule_neg: If net return on property < mortgage rate → leverage destroys value ❌
immobilierVsEtf.s2b_scenario_a_content: Net return on property: 5% | Mortgage rate: 1.5% (2018-2021) | Spread: +3.5% | Result: every borrowed euro earns 3.5 cents more than it costs ✅
immobilierVsEtf.s2b_scenario_b_content: Net return on property: 3% | Mortgage rate: 3.5% (2024-2026) | Spread: -0.5% | Result: every borrowed euro costs 0.5 cents more than it earns ❌ | In this case, you are betting solely on the future capital gain of the property.
immobilierVsEtf.s2b_p2: Between 2015 and 2021, Belgian mortgage rates oscillated between 1% and 2%. Real estate leverage was then exceptionally powerful — borrowing at 1.5% to generate 5% net represents a spread of 3.5 points.
immobilierVsEtf.s2b_p3: Since 2022, rates have risen sharply. In 2026, a Belgian mortgage is negotiated between 3% and 4% depending on the profile. With net rental yields often between 2.5% and 3.5% in major Belgian cities, the spread has become neutral or even negative.
immobilierVsEtf.s2b_p4: This reversal doesn't mean real estate is a bad idea — but it means the thesis of « magic leverage that always beats ETFs » is much less obvious than it was 5 years ago.
immobilierVsEtf.s2b_tip: Before any rental real estate project, calculate your real net return (rent - charges - vacancies - maintenance - property tax) and compare it to your credit rate. If the spread is less than 1%, you are essentially betting on the future capital gain of the property — not on the cash flow.

analyseProductsPage.meta_title: Belgian Banking Products Reviewed — ETF Comparisons 2026
analyseProductsPage.meta_description: Honest reviews of Belgian savings and investment products: branch 21, pension funds, term notes. Real costs, net returns, ETF alternatives.
analyseProductsPage.hero_subtitle: Before you sign, understand what you are actually buying. Real costs, net returns, ETF alternatives — no sugarcoating.
analyseProductsPage.intro: Belgian banks and insurers market dozens of savings and investment products. We analyse them one by one — real costs, taxation, and comparison with the ETF alternative.
analyseProductsPage.cat_pension_subtitle: The 30% tax benefit is real — but annual fees and the 8% exit tax radically change the math over 30 years.
analyseProductsPage.cat_insurance_guaranteed_subtitle: An attractive guaranteed rate on the surface — but the 2% premium tax, 8-year lock-in, and inflation often reduce the real return to almost nothing.
analyseProductsPage.cat_insurance_funds_subtitle: The tax wrapper looks appealing — but layered fees (premium tax + contract fees + fund TER) frequently exceed 3.5% per year.
analyseProductsPage.cat_active_funds_subtitle: Pitched to savers with EUR 50,000+, these profiled funds stack entry fees, high TERs, and the Reynders tax — delivering returns that structurally underperform their benchmark.
analyseProductsPage.cat_investment_plans_subtitle: The app looks slick and transaction fees seem zero — but the underlying funds charge 1.5-2% per year. Over 20 years, the gap versus an ETF AutoInvest is substantial.
analyseProductsPage.card_ethias_teaser: 2.90% guaranteed, but -2% from day one. Full fee breakdown.
analyseProductsPage.card_kbc_teaser: The 30% tax benefit hides fees that drain your capital over 30 years.
analyseProductsPage.card_argenta_teaser: Zero entry fee, but annual costs that are heavy compared to ETFs.
analyseProductsPage.card_bnp_teaser: TER 1.38 % + 3 % entry fee. ESG label, but high costs that weigh on net returns.
analyseProductsPage.card_belfius_teaser: TER 1.47 % + 3 % entry fee. High equity exposure, but total costs among the heaviest on the market.
analyseProductsPage.card_ag_teaser: Unit-linked life insurance: 2% premium tax + contract management fees + fund TER. Layered fees frequently exceed 3% per year.
analyseProductsPage.card_ag_safe_teaser: Branch 21: capital guaranteed, withholding tax exempt, but the 2% premium tax eats into every payment. Over 30 years: +€57,200 vs +€241,600 for an ETF.
analyseProductsPage.card_belfius_capital_safe_teaser: Branch 21: guaranteed capital, but 4.5% entry friction — the highest of all Branch 21 products reviewed. Over 30 years: +€50,520 vs +€270,611 for a World ETF.
analyseProductsPage.card_nn_teaser: TER 1.52 % + variable entry fees. Branch 23 with mixed equity-bond exposure.
analyseProductsPage.note: A product you’d like us to review? Write to us: <email>contact@clearinvest.be</email>
analyseProductsPage.card_baloise_teaser: Up to 5.50% total entry friction and ~2.20%/year in fees — all KID-confirmed. The most expensive Branch 23 product in our review.
analyseProductsPage.card_axa_teaser: The only Branch 23 product with passive management. Competitive with an ETF only under the long-term savings scheme — under strict conditions.
analyseProductsPage.card_vivium_teaser: 2.54%/year to invest in ETFs available directly at 0.17%/year. Minimum €25,000.
analyseProductsPage.card_crelan_opps_teaser: Fund-of-funds with 2.80%/year fees. Official net return: 2.57%/year — barely above a regulated savings account.

analyseAgFundPlus.meta_title: AG Fund + Branch 23 Review — 4.5% Entry Costs for What Benefit?
analyseAgFundPlus.meta_description: Full review of AG Fund + Branch 23. Why Branch 23 has never offered a real tax advantage over accumulator ETFs — and delivers up to 2.6 times less than what you could have built.
analyseAgFundPlus.hero_title: AG Fund + — 4.5% Entry Fees for a Now Useless Wrapper
analyseAgFundPlus.hero_subtitle: AG Insurance relies on the Branch 23 tax wrapper to justify its 4.5% entry friction. But for an investor in accumulator ETFs, this wrapper provides zero tax advantage — only significantly higher costs.
analyseAgFundPlus.tldr_text: AG Fund + charges 2% premium tax + 2.5% entry fees = 4.5% total entry friction. To justify such costs, AG Insurance relies on the Branch 23 tax wrapper. Problem: for an investor in accumulator ETFs, this advantage does not exist. Tax treatment is identical (same 10% capital gains tax), but AG costs you €168,000 in missed profits over 30 years.
analyseAgFundPlus.intro_p1: AG Insurance distributes its 'AG Fund +' product through a wide network of brokers and partner banks (including BNP Paribas Fortis). The promise is that of professionally diversified management (the 'Rainbow' funds) within the protective framework of life insurance.
analyseAgFundPlus.intro_p2: However, this distribution structure has a massive cost: entry fees that are among the highest on the Belgian market, for a wrapper whose tax interest is now almost zero.
analyseAgFundPlus.s1_val_availability: Redemptions possible (1% exit fee if < 3 years)
analyseAgFundPlus.s1_val_cgt: 10% on gains (> €10,000/year) — exempt under long-term savings scheme (conditions apply)
analyseAgFundPlus.s1_source: Fees shown are based on AG Insurance Key Information Documents (KID/DICI) available on the FSMA website. The 2.50% entry fee and ~1.70%/year are standard rates — some brokers may apply different tariffs.
analyseAgFundPlus.s2_title: The Branch 23 Tax Advantage — A Myth for ETF Investors
analyseAgFundPlus.s2_p1: The AG Fund + pitch relies on the exemption from withholding tax. This is a technically correct but irrelevant argument for a modern investor: accumulator ETFs like IMIE do not distribute dividends and therefore incur no withholding tax either.
analyseAgFundPlus.s2_p2: The treatment of capital gains is identical between Branch 23 and accumulator ETFs. Branch 23 has never been 'more tax-efficient' than an accumulator ETF — it is simply much more expensive due to its 4.5% entry friction and high management fees.
analyseAgFundPlus.s2_p3: The right question is not about taxation. It is: why pay 4.5% at entry and 1.70%/year in fees for a tax treatment identical to an ETF at 0.17%/year?
analyseAgFundPlus.s2_warning: Non-fiscal Branch 23 has never offered a real tax advantage over an accumulator ETF like IMIE. Not before, not now. What you are paying for is an insurance wrapper that costs 8 to 10 times more — with no tax benefit in return.
analyseAgFundPlus.s3_point1_title: Premium tax: 2% — The Legal Entry Price
analyseAgFundPlus.s3_point1_desc: Identical to all Branch 23 products. Levied on each payment. Over 30 years at €300/month, this represents €2,160 in tax alone.
analyseAgFundPlus.s3_point2_desc: Unlike the tax, these fees are a commercial choice of AG. Combined with the tax, you need about 8.7 months just to recover your initial stake before generating a single euro of real return.
analyseAgFundPlus.s3_point3_title: Double layer of annual fees: ~1.40% to 1.95%
analyseAgFundPlus.s3_point3_desc: AG contract fees (~0.70%) are added to the TER of the 'Rainbow' funds (~1.00%). On €100,000, this represents up to €1,950/year in fees, compared to €170/year for an ETF.
analyseAgFundPlus.s3_warning: ⚠ AG's 'Rainbow' funds are not index ETFs — they are active funds whose TER is added to the insurance contract fees. The net performance of the whole rarely reflects that of the global market.
analyseAgFundPlus.s4_intro: Comparison based on a payment of €300/month (€3,600/year) for 30 years. Total invested: €108,000. We use the same figures as for NN Strategy to allow direct comparison.
analyseAgFundPlus.s4_callout: AG Fund + generates ~2.6 times more net profit (+€102,590 vs +€270,611) — a gap of €168,021.
analyseAgFundPlus.s4_context_note: Within the Branch 23 universe: NN Strategy (+€115,140 net) is structurally cheaper than AG Fund + (+€102,590), mainly due to lower entry fees. Both lose massively to the ETF.
analyseAgFundPlus.s4_nuance_note: Note: the ETF simulation applies CGT as if all the capital were sold in one go. In practice, progressive selling reduces real CGT to almost zero — making the gap even more favorable to the ETF.
analyseAgFundPlus.s4_methodology_note: Methodology note: the 8% return used for IMIE is based on the historical average of the MSCI ACWI IMI over 20 years (2005–2025). The net return of 6.30% for AG Fund + assumes total fees of 1.70%/year — at the bottom of the observed range. The comparison is therefore conservative and favourable to AG Fund +.
analyseAgFundPlus.s4_cta_text: Simulate with your own numbers in our calculator.
analyseAgFundPlus.s5_p1: Branch 23 allows designating specific beneficiaries outside classic succession rules. AG Insurance being the leading insurer in Belgium, this mechanism is perfectly established. For an investor over 60 looking to organize their succession, this legal advantage is real.
analyseAgFundPlus.s5_p2: AG Insurance distributes its products through banks (BNP Paribas Fortis) and brokers. The 2.5% entry fees remunerate this network. You pay the price for the convenience of not opening a broker account dearly: €168,000 in unrealized profits over 30 years.
analyseAgFundPlus.s5_callout: Branch 23 remains a legitimate estate planning tool for built-up wealth. But for the wealth accumulation phase, the AG Fund + wrapper represents a cost without any real tax counterpart compared to a direct ETF.
analyseAgFundPlus.s6_verdict_p1: AG Fund + is the most expensive Branch 23 product in our analysis. The insurance wrapper offers no measurable tax advantage over an accumulator ETF — it never has. What remains: 4.5% entry friction and ~1.70%/year in fees for an asset you can buy directly at 0.17%/year.
analyseAgFundPlus.s6_verdict_p2: The product is not fraudulent, but it is structurally 8 to 10 times more expensive than a direct ETF investment for an identically identical tax treatment. You are paying for an insurance wrapper that provides no measurable benefit.
analyseAgFundPlus.s6_verdict_p3: If you subscribed recently (less than 3 years), check the surrender fees (1%) before cancelling — but beyond 3 years, the switch to ETF is mathematically justified in almost all cases.
analyseAgFundPlus.s6_ideal_desc: Estate planning for investors 60+, objective of targeted transmission outside classic succession.
analyseAgFundPlus.s6_avoid_desc: Any investor seeking to build up wealth. The 4.5% entry friction and high annual fees have never had a real tax counterpart compared to an ETF.
analyseAgFundPlus.disclaimer: Illustrative simulation only. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.
analyseAgFundPlus.s5_lts_p1: Under the long-term savings scheme, Branch 23 contracts benefit from a 30% tax reduction on premiums (up to €2,450/year) and a capital gains tax exemption from 2026, subject to access conditions for the federal tax basket (recent mortgage or no mortgage).
analyseAgFundPlus.s5_lts_p2: Does this change the verdict for AG Fund+?
analyseAgFundPlus.s5_lts_p3: No — the 4.5% entry friction alone is enough to invalidate the calculation.
analyseAgFundPlus.s5_lts_p4: 30-year simulations show that for the 30% tax benefit to offset the costs of a Branch 23 product, the entry friction must stay around 3% maximum, annual fees must be ≤ ~1.4%/year, and the product must track an index. AG Fund+ combines a 4.5% entry friction (the highest in our analysis), annual fees of ~1.7%/year, and active management whose gross return is structurally below the market. These three combined negative factors far exceed what the 30% tax refund can compensate — even under the most favourable conditions.

analyseBelfiusPension.hero_title: Belfius Pension Fund High Equities — The Tax Benefit Doesn't Offset the Fees
analyseBelfiusPension.hero_subtitle: 2% entry fee + 1.31% TER + 8% exit tax at 60 — the pension savings tax benefit is real, but not enough to erase the structural underperformance vs a World ETF.
analyseBelfiusPension.tldr_text: Belfius Pension Fund High Equities generates a net gain of +€35,630 over 30 years vs +€91,652 for a World ETF — even after accounting for the 30% tax benefit and ETF taxation. The optimal strategy: max out pension savings for the tax benefit, invest everything else in ETFs.
analyseBelfiusPension.intro_p1: Belgian pension savings offer a genuine tax benefit: a 30% tax reduction on annual contributions up to €1,050. That's the central argument Belfius uses for its High Equities fund, positioned as the most dynamic option in their pension range.
analyseBelfiusPension.intro_p2: But the tax benefit alone cannot compensate for the combination of a 2% entry fee, a 1.31% TER, and an 8% exit tax at age 60. Over 30 years, the gap versus a World ETF remains substantial.
analyseBelfiusPension.s2_p1: The total cost of Belfius Pension Fund High Equities breaks down into three distinct layers that compound over time.
analyseBelfiusPension.s2_entry_desc: Deducted on every contribution. At €1,050/year over 30 years, that's €630 lost immediately before any investment growth begins.
analyseBelfiusPension.s2_ter_desc: Annual management fees do most of the damage. On a €50,000 portfolio, that's €655/year that doesn't compound — versus €85/year for an equivalent ETF.
analyseBelfiusPension.s2_effect_desc: Compounding fee drag: every euro in annual fees costs multiple euros in final capital — the impact amplifies over time.
analyseBelfiusPension.s2_warning: ⚠ Belfius High Equities' 1.31% TER is among the highest of Belgian pension savings funds. Over 30 years, the net return gap (4.40% vs 8%) represents a missed gain of over €56,000.
analyseBelfiusPension.s3_p1: For an objective comparison, we start with an annual contribution of €1,050 (the 2026 tax cap), totalling €31,500 invested over 30 years.
analyseBelfiusPension.s3_p2: The tax benefit is genuine: €315/year tax reduction = €9,450 cumulated over 30 years. But this must be weighed against the 8% exit tax and the return drag from annual fees.
analyseBelfiusPension.s3_p3: The 8% exit tax is applied to the full accumulated capital at age 60 — including initial contributions. In our simulation this amounts to approximately €5,440, or more than half the cumulated tax benefit.
analyseBelfiusPension.s3_example_p5: Real net gain: +€35,630 (vs +€91,652 for the ETF)
analyseBelfiusPension.s3_p4: Even including the tax benefit, the Belfius fund generates less than half the net gain of a World ETF over the same period.
analyseBelfiusPension.s3_etf_flex_text: A couple can each benefit from the €10,000 annual ETF capital gains exemption. By selling gradually in retirement, real ETF taxation can be reduced to near zero — making the comparison even more favourable for the ETF.
analyseBelfiusPension.s4_intro: Simulation based on €1,050/year (2026 pension savings tax cap) over 30 years. Total invested: €31,500. Reference gross return: 8%/year (MSCI ACWI IMI historical average).
analyseBelfiusPension.s4_note: Even with the tax benefit, the ETF generates +€58,652 more net gain over 30 years.
analyseBelfiusPension.s4_context_note: Comparison within the pension savings universe: KBC Pricos and Argenta Pensioenspaarfonds have lower TERs than Belfius High Equities (1.31%). If you keep a pension fund, choose the cheapest one.
analyseBelfiusPension.s4_methodology_note: Methodology note: the 8% gross return is based on the MSCI ACWI IMI historical average (2005–2025). The 4.40% net return for Belfius factors in entry fees amortised over 30 years and the 1.31% TER. ETF taxation applies the 10% levy as if all capital is sold at once — gradual selling reduces real taxation.
analyseBelfiusPension.s4_cta_text: Run the numbers with your own figures in our calculator.
analyseBelfiusPension.s_hybrid_title: The optimal strategy: pension savings + ETF
analyseBelfiusPension.s_hybrid_intro: Pension savings shouldn't be dismissed entirely. The 30% tax benefit is real and immediate. The rational strategy: max out pension savings to the cap (€1,050/year) to capture the tax benefit, then invest everything else in ETFs. You get the tax advantage without concentrating all your savings in a high-fee fund.
analyseBelfiusPension.s_hybrid_gap_note: The ETF generates 2.6× more net gain, even after taxation.
analyseBelfiusPension.s_hybrid_why_title: Why the hybrid strategy is most rational
analyseBelfiusPension.s_hybrid_why_p: With €1,050/year in pension savings, you get €315 in immediate tax reduction — a guaranteed 30% return on that contribution, regardless of markets. For every additional euro, an ETF is mathematically superior. Never put more than the tax cap into a pension fund.
analyseBelfiusPension.s_hybrid_conclusion: Max out pension savings to capture the tax benefit. Invest the rest in ETFs to maximise your long-term wealth.
analyseBelfiusPension.s6_step1_heading: If you don't yet have a pension fund
analyseBelfiusPension.s6_step1_desc: Open a pension fund with the cheapest provider (Argenta or KBC Pricos — TER < 1%), contribute exactly €1,050/year, and invest the rest of your savings in ETFs via a low-cost broker.
analyseBelfiusPension.s6_step2_desc: The 1.31% TER is high, but switching between pension funds incurs costs. Assess transfer fees before acting — but for new contributions, consider a cheaper fund.
analyseBelfiusPension.s6_summary: Simple rule: €1,050/year in pension savings (any fund, as cheap as possible) + everything else in ETFs. Never more than the tax cap in a pension fund.
analyseBelfiusPension.s7_verdict_p1: Belfius Pension Fund High Equities isn't a terrible product in isolation — the 30% tax benefit is real and justifies using it up to the cap. But its 1.31% TER is among the highest on the market, and the 8% exit tax eats into a significant portion of the tax advantage.
analyseBelfiusPension.s7_verdict_p2: Over 30 years, the net gain gap versus a World ETF is +€56,022 in favour of the ETF — even after factoring in the tax benefit and ETF taxation.
analyseBelfiusPension.s7_ideal_desc: Up to €1,050/year to capture the 30% tax benefit. Prefer a fund with a lower TER if you have the option.
analyseBelfiusPension.s7_avoid_desc: Investing beyond the tax cap in this fund, or treating it as a replacement for ETFs for the non-pension portion of your savings.

analyseBelfius.hero_title: Belfius Pension Fund High Equities — The Tax Benefit Doesn’t Offset the Fees
analyseBelfius.hero_subtitle: 3% entry fee + 1.49% TER + 8% exit tax at 60 — the pension savings tax benefit is real, but not enough to erase the structural underperformance vs a World ETF.
analyseBelfius.tldr_text: Belfius Pension Fund High Equities generates +€33,000 over 30 years of contributions at the tax maximum (€1,050/year) vs +€91,652 for a Global ETF — even after the 30% tax benefit and ETF taxation. Its 1.49% TER and 3% entry fee weigh heavy — the 30-year lock-in still does considerable damage.
analyseBelfius.intro_p1: Belgian pension savings offer a genuine tax benefit: a 30% tax reduction on annual contributions up to €1,050. That’s the central argument Belfius uses for its High Equities fund, positioned as the most dynamic option in their pension range.
analyseBelfius.intro_p2: But the tax benefit alone cannot compensate for the combination of a 3% entry fee, a 1.49% TER, and an 8% exit tax at age 60. Over 30 years, the gap versus a World ETF remains substantial.
analyseBelfius.s2_p1: To understand why this product underperforms, you need to look at the layered fee structure:
analyseBelfius.s2_entry_desc: Deducted on every contribution. At €1,050/year over 30 years, that’s €945 lost immediately before any investment growth begins.
analyseBelfius.s2_ter_desc: Annual management fees do most of the damage. On a €50,000 portfolio, that’s €745/year that doesn’t compound — versus €85/year for an equivalent ETF.
analyseBelfius.s2_effect_desc: Every euro in annual fees costs multiple euros in final capital — the impact amplifies over time. The real cost of a 1.49% TER over 30 years is far greater than 1.49% × 30 due to the compounding effect.
analyseBelfius.s2_warning: ⚠ Belfius High Equities’ 1.49% TER remains 8.8× higher than a World ETF’s TER (0.17%). Over 30 years, the net return gap (4.20% vs 8%) represents a missed gain of over €58,000.
analyseBelfius.s3_p1: For an objective comparison, we start with an annual contribution of €1,050 (the 2026 tax cap), totalling €31,500 invested over 30 years.
analyseBelfius.s3_p2: The tax benefit is genuine: €315/year tax reduction = €9,450 cumulated over 30 years. But this must be weighed against the 8% exit tax and the return drag from annual fees.
analyseBelfius.s3_p3: The 8% exit tax is applied to the full accumulated capital at age 60 — including initial contributions. In our simulation this amounts to approximately €5,440, or more than half the cumulated tax benefit.
analyseBelfius.s3_example_p5: Real net gain: +€33,000 (vs +€91,652 for the ETF)
analyseBelfius.s3_p4: Even including the tax benefit, the Belfius fund generates less than half the net gain of a World ETF over the same period.
analyseBelfius.s3_etf_flex_text: Unlike the pension savings exit tax of 8% (levied in one shot, unavoidably, at age 60), the 10% ETF capital gains tax offers genuine flexibility: by selling gradually over several years in retirement, you benefit each year from the €10,000 exemption. In practice, your real effective rate can fall to 2–3% — making the comparison even more favourable to ETFs than the figures in the next section suggest.
analyseBelfius.s4_intro: Let’s compare the mathematical reality over 30 years, using the €1,050/year tax cap. Total paid out of pocket: €31,500.
analyseBelfius.s4_note: The World ETF generates 2.8× more net gain — even accounting for the 2026 capital gains tax and the absence of a tax benefit.
analyseBelfius.s4_peer_title: Comparison within the pension savings universe
analyseBelfius.s4_peer_note: Belfius now has the highest entry fees (3%) and a TER of 1.49% — above Argenta (1.44%). Argenta (0% entry, 1.44% TER) remains the cheapest. The reightly higher TER.
analyseBelfius.s4_methodology_note: * Methodology note: the 8% return is based on the MSCI ACWI IMI historical average over 2005–2025. Over the last 10 years, IMIE actually delivered ~10–11%/year. We use 8% to stay consistent with our long-term projections. This comparison is therefore favourable to Belfius.
analyseBelfius.s4_cta_text: Run the numbers with your own figures in our calculator — Belfius Pension Fund is included as a comparison product.
analyseBelfius.s_hybrid_title: The Hybrid Strategy — A Mathematical Myth
analyseBelfius.s_hybrid_intro: This is the most sophisticated argument for pension savings: contribute €1,050/year to Belfius, reclaim €315 in tax, and reinvest it in a World ETF. You combine the tax bonus and ETF performance. Here’s what the numbers actually show.
analyseBelfius.s_hybrid_gap_note: Gap: +€58,652 in favour of the pure ETF.
analyseBelfius.s_hybrid_why_p: The net bonus from Belfius is €294/year (€315 minus €21 in entry fees). This amount is fixed. The return gap between the ETF and Belfius (8% vs 4.40%), on the other hand, applies to a capital base that grows every year. The compounding effect eventually crushes the bonus — by year 5 or 6 for most savers.
analyseBelfius.s_hybrid_conclusion: The €315/year bonus is real. But it’s fixed and doesn’t compound. The return gap between a World ETF and Belfius does compound — and overtakes the bonus around year 5.
analyseBelfius.s5_title: Pension savings: a dead end for most
analyseBelfius.s5_intro: The numbers speak clearly: for the vast majority of savers, the Belfius Pension Fund is a financial dead end compared to passive investing.
analyseBelfius.s5_point1: The tax benefit is a fixed 30% bonus (max €315/year) that doesn’t compound — unlike fees, which accumulate every year on your entire portfolio.
analyseBelfius.s5_point2: Even the ‘hybrid’ strategy (pension savings up to the cap + ETF for the rest) loses against a pure ETF over 30 years. ETF growth surplus overtakes the tax bonus by year 5–6.
analyseBelfius.s5_point3: Your capital is locked until age 60. Every euro in this fund is a euro that can’t compound freely.
analyseBelfius.s5_outro: If you’re under 50, the Belfius Pension Fund structurally underperforms a simple World ETF — even after the tax benefit. If you’re set on opening a pension savings fund, choose Argenta (0% entry fee) — never Belfius.
analyseBelfius.s5_link: → See the Argenta analysis (0% entry fee)
analyseBelfius.s6_verdict_p1: Belfius Pension Fund High Equities isn’t a terrible product in isolation — the 30% tax benefit is real and justifies using it up to the cap. With a 1.49% TER and 3% entry fees, it is more expensive than Argenta over the long term.
analyseBelfius.s6_verdict_p2: But the 3% entry fee — identical to KBC — and the 8% exit tax significantly erode this advantage. Over 30 years, the net gain gap vs a World ETF is +€58,652 in favour of the ETF, even after the tax benefit.
analyseBelfius.s6_ideal_desc: Investors aged 50+ with a very short horizon (less than 10 years before age 60) — and only via Argenta, not Belfius.
analyseBelfius.s6_avoid_desc: Anyone under 50. And for any amount beyond the €1,050/year tax cap.
analyseBelfius.s7_title: I already have Belfius Pension — what now?
analyseBelfius.s7_step1_heading: Stop new contributions to Belfius immediately.
analyseBelfius.s7_step1_desc: Every euro contributed today still pays a 3% entry fee and feeds a 1.49%/year cost machine. The €315 tax benefit does not offset this cost over the long term.
analyseBelfius.s7_step2_heading: Redirect your €1,050/year to Argenta or a World ETF.
analyseBelfius.s7_step2_desc: Argenta (0% entry fee, TER ~1.44%) is preferable to Belfius if you want to keep contributing to pension savings. Or open an account with MeDirect or Saxo Bank to invest in ETFs — no entry fee, no lock-in until age 60.
analyseBelfius.s7_summary: In short: leave the money already invested alone. But don’t put another cent in.
analyseBelfius.disclaimer: This content is provided for informational purposes only and does not constitute investment advice within the meaning of MiFID II. Simulations are based on historical assumptions and do not guarantee future performance.
analyseBelfius.s_hybrid_horizon_desc: For investors aged 50 and over, with a short horizon (10–15 years). The crossover point has not yet been reached and the 294 €/year bonus still exceeds the ETF growth gap. In that case, use Argenta (0% entry) rather than Belfius.
analyseBelfius.chart_caption: Belfius has 3% entry fees — the highest of the three funds — and a 1.49% TER.
analyseBelfius.s_caf_title: The Cafeteria Plan — The Only Serious Exception
analyseBelfius.s_caf_product_warning: ⚠️ The analysis below uses Argenta ARPE figures (0% entry fee, ~1.40%/year) to establish the most favourable case for pension savings. Belfius Pension Fund (3% entry fee, 1.49%/year) produces an even less favourable result: the margin in favour of the ETF is even wider.
analyseBelfius.s_caf_intro_p1: If your employer offers a cafeteria plan, you can fund your pension savings with gross euros. This is the only scenario where pension savings becomes mathematically competitive — thanks to a triple simultaneous subsidy: exemption from NSSO contributions (13.07%), gross employer budget, and a 30% tax reduction from the State.
analyseBelfius.s_caf_intro_p2: Let’s see whether this subsidy is sufficient to reverse the conclusion.
analyseBelfius.s_caf_caveat: This analysis applies only to employees whose employer offers a cafeteria plan — mainly large private-sector companies. It assumes a marginal tax rate of 50% (taxable income > ~€46,440/year in 2026). At lower rates, the figures are less favourable for pension savings.
analyseBelfius.s_caf_meca_title: The Mechanics: What Happens to €1,050 Gross?
analyseBelfius.s_caf_meca_explanation: Investor A receives €457 net and invests it in an ETF. Investor B puts €1,050 into pension savings, but their net salary decreases by €210 (€525 BIK tax minus €315 recovered tax reduction).
analyseBelfius.s_caf_compare_title: Same Net Sacrifice: Who Wins Over 30 Years?
analyseBelfius.s_caf_compare_intro: For a fair comparison, we start from the same net sacrifice: €210/year less in the bank account in both cases. Investor A: net bonus of €457 + €210 additional from salary = €667/year in a World ETF. Investor B: cafeteria plan = €1,050/year in ARPE, for a net cost of €210/year.
analyseBelfius.s_caf_callout: Even with the cafeteria plan’s triple advantage — €1,050 invested versus just €667 for the ETF — the World ETF generates ~€9,276 more over 30 years. The ~1.40%/year in annual fees erases the subsidy.
analyseBelfius.s_caf_context_note: Without a cafeteria plan, the gap was ~€53,000 in the ETF’s favour. The cafeteria plan reduces this gap to ~€9,276 — without reversing it. That is significant, but insufficient.
analyseBelfius.s_caf_concl_title: The Cafeteria Plan: Useful, But Not Enough
analyseBelfius.s_caf_concl_p1: The cafeteria plan is real and powerful. It transforms a clearly losing product into something almost competitive. But ‘almost’ is not ‘better’.
analyseBelfius.s_caf_concl_p2: Over 30 years, with the same net sacrifice of €210/year, the World ETF still leads — by ~€9,276 versus Argenta ARPE (the best fund on the market), and by an even wider margin versus Belfius Pension Fund, where the 3% entry fee and 1.49% TER make the result worse.
analyseBelfius.s_caf_concl_p3: If your employer offers a cafeteria plan and you want to use it for pension savings, Argenta (0% entry fee) remains the only fund worth considering in this context. KBC, BNP, and Belfius make the outcome even worse with their higher entry fees and TERs.
analyseBelfius.s_caf_concl_p4: But the conclusion is the same with or without a cafeteria plan: over a long horizon, the World ETF produces more net wealth for the same financial effort.
analyseBelfius.s_caf_methodology: Methodology note: calculations based on a 50% marginal tax rate (taxable income > ~€46,440/year in 2026) and an employee NSSO rate of 13.07%. BIK taxed at the marginal rate. ARPE return: 4.5% net (Argenta — best fund on the market). ETF return: 8%/year (historical average MSCI ACWI IMI 2005–2025). CGT calculated on a single sale — in practice, progressive selling reduces the ETF CGT to near zero, making the ETF even more advantageous.
analyseBelfius.s_caf_disclaimer: Indicative simulation. Past performance is not a guarantee of future returns.

analyseNnStrategy.meta_title: NN Strategy Branch 23 Review — The Tax Illusion at 1.50%/year
analyseNnStrategy.meta_description: Full review of NN Strategy Branch 23. Why Branch 23 has never offered a real tax advantage over accumulator ETFs — and costs 8 times more.
analyseNnStrategy.hero_title: NN Strategy — A Costly ETF in a Useless Wrapper
analyseNnStrategy.hero_subtitle: Branch 23 is often presented as a tax-efficient haven for Belgian investors. But for those using accumulator ETFs, this advantage is a myth: the tax treatment is identical, while the fees are 8 times higher.
analyseNnStrategy.tldr_text: NN Strategy combines a 2% premium tax on each payment and ~1.50%/year total fees (wrapper + underlying funds). Branch 23 is often sold as a tax haven, but for an investor in accumulator ETFs like IMIE, this advantage is a myth: tax treatment is identical (no withholding tax, same 10% capital gains tax), but with fees 8 times higher. Over 30 years at €300/month, choosing NN Strategy over a World ETF costs you ~€155,500 in unrealised profits — using conservative assumptions.
analyseNnStrategy.s1_val_cgt: 10% on gains (> €10,000/year) — exempt under long-term savings scheme (conditions apply)
analyseNnStrategy.s1_source: Fees shown are based on the Key Information Documents (KID/DICI) available on the FSMA website and NN Insurance Belgium product documentation. Verify the current KID before subscribing.
analyseNnStrategy.s2_title: The Branch 23 Tax Advantage — A Myth for ETF Investors
analyseNnStrategy.s2_p1: The Branch 23 pitch relies on one claim: exemption from withholding tax (précompte mobilier) and (before 2026) from capital gains tax.
analyseNnStrategy.s2_p2: This claim is technically true — but irrelevant for anyone investing in accumulator ETFs like IMIE. Since they do not distribute dividends, there is no withholding tax to avoid. And regarding the 10% capital gains tax, it now applies equally to both solutions.
analyseNnStrategy.s2_p3: Branch 23 therefore offers no real tax advantage over IMIE. It only offers the appearance of one to investors who compare it to savings accounts or distributing funds. The right question is not about taxes — it is: why pay 1.50%/year in fees for a tax treatment identical to what you get at 0.17%/year?
analyseNnStrategy.s2_warning: Non-fiscal Branch 23 has never offered a real tax advantage over an accumulator ETF like IMIE. Not before, not now. What you are paying for is an insurance wrapper that costs 8 to 10 times more — with no tax benefit in return.
analyseNnStrategy.s3_point1_desc: Each euro paid loses 2% immediately by law. On €300/month, €6 disappears every month before even being invested. Over 30 years, this represents €2,160 lost only in premium tax.
analyseNnStrategy.s3_recovery_note: At ~6.50% net/year, it takes ~5.5 months of returns just to recover your initial stake after the 3% entry friction (tax + fees).
analyseNnStrategy.s3_point2_desc: NN charges fees for the administrative management of the contract and the designation of beneficiaries. These fees apply to your total capital each year, regardless of return.
analyseNnStrategy.s3_point3_desc: The chosen funds (active or passive) have their own TER which is added to the NN fees. You quickly reach a total of 1.50% to 1.80%, compared to 0.17% for a direct World ETF.
analyseNnStrategy.s3_warning: ⚠ The total cost of NN Strategy is not displayed in a single line. The wrapper fees and the underlying fund fees add up — and are often not presented together during subscription.
analyseNnStrategy.s4_intro: Comparison based on a payment of €300/month (€3,600/year) for 30 years. Total invested: €108,000.
analyseNnStrategy.s4_callout: The World ETF generates ~2.3 times more net profit (+€270,611 vs +€115,140) — a gap of €155,471.
analyseNnStrategy.s4_nuance_note: Note: the ETF simulation applies the CGT as if all the capital were sold all at once at the end. In practice, by selling progressively at retirement (in annual tranches of €10,000 of capital gains), the ETF investor can reduce the real CGT to almost zero. The real gap is therefore even more favorable to the ETF.
analyseNnStrategy.s4_methodology_note: Methodology note: the 8% return used for IMIE is based on the historical average of the MSCI ACWI IMI over 20 years (2005–2025). The net return of 6.50% for NN Strategy assumes total fees of 1.50%/year — at the bottom of the observed range. The comparison is therefore conservative and favorable to NN Strategy.
analyseNnStrategy.s4_cta_text: Simulate with your own numbers in our calculator.
analyseNnStrategy.s5_p1: A Branch 23 contract allows you to designate specific beneficiaries who receive the capital directly in case of death, bypassing standard inheritance procedure. For investors over 60 who have already built up wealth and wish to plan its transmission outside classic rules, this legal advantage has real value.
analyseNnStrategy.s5_p2: However, this need for transmission does not justify paying 1.50% fees per year for 30 years during the wealth accumulation phase. It is an instrument of transfer, not investment.
analyseNnStrategy.s5_callout: Branch 23 remains a legitimate estate planning tool. But it is a legal instrument, not an investment instrument. If you are looking to transfer built-up wealth, consult a notary — not a bank advisor who will sell you funds at 1.50%/year.
analyseNnStrategy.s6_verdict_p1: NN Strategy is often presented as a tax-efficient wrapper, but the advantage over accumulator ETFs has never existed. Not before, not now. What remains is a 2% premium tax and ~1.50%/year in fees for an asset you can buy directly for 0.17%/year.
analyseNnStrategy.s6_verdict_p2: The product is not fraudulent, but it is structurally 8 to 10 times more expensive than a direct ETF investment for an identically identical tax treatment. You are paying for an insurance wrapper that provides no measurable benefit.
analyseNnStrategy.s6_verdict_p3: If you subscribed recently (less than 5 years), check the surrender fees before cancelling — they can exceed the cost of staying.
analyseNnStrategy.s6_ideal_desc: Estate planning for investors aged 60+, built-up wealth, objective of transmission outside classic succession. In this case: consult a notary.
analyseNnStrategy.s6_avoid_desc: Any investor seeking to build up wealth. The insurance wrapper does not compensate for its costs with any real tax advantage over a direct ETF.
analyseNnStrategy.disclaimer: Simulation for illustrative purposes only. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.
analyseNnStrategy.s5_lts_p1: Under the long-term savings scheme (a separate federal tax scheme distinct from pension savings), Branch 23 contracts benefit from a 30% tax reduction on premiums up to €2,450/year and a permanent capital gains tax exemption from 2026. The scheme is available to taxpayers whose mortgage was taken out recently (post-2016 in Flanders, post-2017 in Brussels) or who have no mortgage.
analyseNnStrategy.s5_lts_p2: Does this change the verdict for NN Strategy?
analyseNnStrategy.s5_lts_p4: First, NN Strategy’s total fees (~1.5%/year) sit at the upper boundary of the threshold beyond which the tax benefit no longer compensates for the cost differential over 30 years. Simulations show that at ~1.4–1.5%/year in fees, the outcome is at best comparable to an ETF — not better.
analyseNnStrategy.s5_lts_p5: Second, and more importantly, NN Strategy’s underlying funds are actively managed. Active funds structurally underperform their benchmark over the long term — between 85% and 92% of active funds underperform their benchmark over 20 years after fees. This gross return deficit is on top of the high fees. The tax benefit cannot compensate for both high fees AND gross returns below the market.
analyseNnStrategy.s5_lts_p6: For long-term savings Branch 23 to be competitive with an ETF, two conditions must be met simultaneously: fees ≤ ~1.4%/year AND an index product delivering gross returns close to the market. NN Strategy meets neither condition with any certainty.

kbcPricosAnalysis.hero_title: KBC Pricos — is the 30% tax benefit really worth it?
kbcPricosAnalysis.hero_subtitle: Belgium's most popular pension savings fund. Let's unpack what the brochure doesn't say.
kbcPricosAnalysis.intro_p1: Your banker's pitch is compelling: invest 1 050 € per year, the State refunds you 315 € via your taxes — an immediate return of 30%, guaranteed.
kbcPricosAnalysis.intro_p2: That's technically true in the first year. But pension savings is a 30–40 year marathon. And over that time, KBC Asset Management's fees do a quiet and devastating job.
kbcPricosAnalysis.s2_p1: To understand why this product underperforms, you need to look at the stacking of fees:
kbcPricosAnalysis.s2_entry_desc: On a payment of 1 050 €, KBC immediately takes 21 €. Only 1 029 € is actually invested. You start each year with a return of -2%.
kbcPricosAnalysis.s2_ter_desc: The silent killer. These fees apply every year to your entire accumulated capital — not just your new contributions.
kbcPricosAnalysis.s2_effect_desc: The effect over 30 years: on a capital of 60 000 €, 1.53% represents 918 € in annual fees. Fees that would themselves have compounded at 8% if invested in an ETF.
kbcPricosAnalysis.s2_warning: ⚠ The cumulative effect of fees over 30 years is non-linear. A TER of 1.53% does not reduce your final capital by 1.53% × 30 = 46%. It reduces it by more than 60% compared to an ETF at 0.17% TER, due to the effect of compound interest.
kbcPricosAnalysis.s3_title: The final blow: the exit tax at age 60
kbcPricosAnalysis.s3_p1: The least-known feature of Belgian pension savings: the 8% advance levy taken at age 60 is not calculated on your actual capital.
kbcPricosAnalysis.s3_p2: The State calculates it on a notional capital, assuming your fund has returned 4.75% per year from day one.
kbcPricosAnalysis.s3_p3: The problem: KBC Pricos often struggles to reach 4.75% net of fees. You therefore pay 8% tax on gains you may never have actually realised.
kbcPricosAnalysis.s3_example_p5: Effective tax rate on your real gains: ~29%
kbcPricosAnalysis.s3_p4: The more your fund underperforms relative to the notional rate of 4.75%, the more punitive your effective tax rate.
kbcPricosAnalysis.s4_intro: Let's compare the mathematical reality over 30 years, with the tax cap of 1 050 €/year (87.50 €/month). Total paid out of pocket: 31 500 €.
kbcPricosAnalysis.s4_note: The Global ETF generates more than double the real profits — even accounting for the new 2026 capital gains tax and the absence of a tax benefit.
kbcPricosAnalysis.s4_methodology_note:
* Methodological note: KBC Pricos's 4.3% return is based on its last 10 years, an exceptionally favourable decade for equity markets. Over that same period, IMIE actually delivered ~10–11%/year — well above the 8% we use here. We use 8% to remain consistent with our long-term projections (20 years), which smooth out bull and bear cycles.

In other words: this comparison favours KBC Pricos. Based on actual returns over the last 10 years, the gap would be even wider.
kbcPricosAnalysis.s4_cta_text: Simulate with your own numbers in our calculator — KBC Pricos is included as a comparison product.
kbcPricosAnalysis.s5_title: Pension savings: a dead end for most
kbcPricosAnalysis.s5_intro: The numbers are clear: for the vast majority of savers, KBC Pricos is a financial dead end compared to passive investment.
kbcPricosAnalysis.s5_point1: A double fee handicap: with 2% upfront and ~1.53%/year, KBC takes thousands of euros before your money even starts to grow.
kbcPricosAnalysis.s5_point2: The tax benefit is a fixed bonus of 30% (max 315 €/year) that does not compound — unlike fees, which accumulate every year on your entire savings.
kbcPricosAnalysis.s5_point3: For those under 45, the leverage of annual fees totally destroys the initial tax benefit over the long term.
kbcPricosAnalysis.s5_outro: If you are under 50, KBC Pricos is structurally losing against a simple Global ETF.
kbcPricosAnalysis.s5_link: → See the Argenta analysis (0% entry fees)
kbcPricosAnalysis.s6_verdict_p1: KBC Pricos should be avoided. It is one of the worst-performing pension savings products on the Belgian market due to its aggressive fee stacking.
kbcPricosAnalysis.s6_verdict_p2: The only exception: those over 50–55 with a short horizon (10–15 years). In this specific case, the tax bonus doesn't have time to be entirely consumed by fees. But even in this scenario, Argenta (0% entry) is consistently superior.
kbcPricosAnalysis.s6_ideal_desc: Investors aged 50+ looking for an immediate tax bonus over a very short horizon.
kbcPricosAnalysis.s6_avoid_desc: Anyone under 45. The opportunity cost versus an ETF is massive.
kbcPricosAnalysis.tldr_text: KBC Pricos charges 2% upfront + ~1.41%/year in management fees. Over 30 years of contributions at the tax maximum (€1,050/year), this costs you more than €44,000 compared to a Global ETF — that's 140 times your annual tax benefit of €315. If you are under 45, this product destroys your long-term wealth.
kbcPricosAnalysis.s3_etf_flex_text: Unlike KBC's 8% tax (taken in one shot, unavoidably, at age 60), the 10% capital gains tax on ETFs offers real flexibility: by selling gradually over several years in retirement, you benefit each year from the €10 000 exemption. In practice, your effective real rate can fall to 2–3% — making the comparison even more favourable to ETFs than the figures in the next section show.
kbcPricosAnalysis.chart_caption: From this point, every additional year with KBC costs you more in fees than it earns you in tax benefit.
kbcPricosAnalysis.s7_title: I've already subscribed to KBC Pricos — what now?
kbcPricosAnalysis.s7_step1_heading: Stop all new contributions to KBC Pricos immediately.
kbcPricosAnalysis.s7_step1_desc: Every euro contributed today still pays 2% entry fees + feeds a fee machine of 1.41%/year. The 315 € tax benefit does not compensate for this cost over the long term.
kbcPricosAnalysis.s7_step2_heading: Redirect your 1 050 €/year to a Global ETF via a fee-free broker.
kbcPricosAnalysis.s7_step2_desc: MeDirect or Saxo Bank (AutoInvest) allow you to invest automatically, with no entry fees, with stock exchange tax handled automatically. Your money already at KBC: leave it to grow until age 60 (withdrawing it now costs more than leaving it).
kbcPricosAnalysis.s7_summary: In summary: don't touch the money already invested. But don't put another cent in.
kbcPricosAnalysis.s_hybrid_title: The Hybrid Strategy — A Mathematical Myth
kbcPricosAnalysis.s_hybrid_intro: This is the most sophisticated argument for Pricos: the 'hybrid' strategy. The idea: invest 1 050 €/year with KBC, get back 315 € in tax, and reinvest it in a Global ETF. You capture both the tax bonus AND ETF performance. Note: these 315 € only exist if you choose Pricos. This argument deserves rigorous analysis.
kbcPricosAnalysis.s_hybrid_gap_note: Gap: ~33 700 € in favour of the pure ETF strategy.
kbcPricosAnalysis.s_hybrid_why_title: The illusion of the tax bonus
kbcPricosAnalysis.s_hybrid_why_p: Pricos's net bonus is actually 294 € (315 € minus 21 € in entry fees). This amount is fixed. In contrast, the fee gap (1.53% vs 0.17%) and return difference applies to your total capital, which grows every year. The opportunity cost eventually crushes the bonus.
kbcPricosAnalysis.s_hybrid_horizon_title: An even weaker argument for Pricos
kbcPricosAnalysis.s_hybrid_horizon_desc: Due to the 2% entry fees and a higher TER, the crossover point arrives as early as year 5 or 6. Even for a short horizon, the hybrid argument collapses faster. If you insist on this strategy, at least use Argenta.
kbcPricosAnalysis.s_hybrid_conclusion: The 315 €/year bonus is real. But it is fixed and does not compound. The return gap between a Global ETF and Pricos does compound — and surpasses it around year 5.
kbcPricosAnalysis.s_caf_title: The Cafeteria Plan — The Only Serious Exception
kbcPricosAnalysis.s_caf_product_warning: ⚠️ The analysis below uses Argenta ARPE figures (0% entry fee, ~1.40%/year) to establish the most favourable case for pension savings. KBC Pricos (2% entry fee, 1.53%/year) produces an even less favourable result: the ETF beats KBC Pricos by an even wider margin than the ~€9,276 shown here.

It assumes a marginal tax rate of 50% (taxable income > ~€46,440/year in 2026). At lower rates, the figures are less favourable for pension savings.

kbcPricosAnalysis.s_caf_compare_title: Same Net Sacrifice: Who Wins Over 30 Years?
kbcPricosAnalysis.s_caf_compare_intro: For a fair comparison, we start from the same net sacrifice: €210/year less in the bank account in both cases. Investor A: net bonus of €457 + €210 additional from salary = €667/year in a World ETF. Investor B: cafeteria plan = €1,050/year in ARPE, for a net cost of €210/year.

kbcPricosAnalysis.s_caf_concl_p1: The cafeteria plan is real and powerful. It transforms a clearly losing product into something almost competitive. But ‘almost’ is not ‘better’.
