analyseArgenta.hero_title: Argenta ARPE — is Belgium's top banking option enough against ETFs?
analyseArgenta.hero_subtitle: 0% entry fees, a solid track record. Argenta is Belgium's favourite pension savings fund. Let's see if that's enough.
analyseArgenta.s1_p1: Argenta stands radically apart from its competitors (KBC, Belfius, BNP) with a striking argument: 0% entry fees.
analyseArgenta.s1_p2: Where a classic bank takes 2 to 3% on each contribution, Argenta invests your full amount. On paper, it's the most honest product in the banking sector.
analyseArgenta.s1_box_p1: 30% tax reduction on a maximum of 1 050 €/year.
analyseArgenta.s1_box_p2: The State gives you back 315 € each year. Argenta is unquestionably the best vehicle for capturing this benefit without losing money at the counter.
analyseArgenta.s2_point1_desc: This is the major advantage. Your 1 050 € works in full from day one.
analyseArgenta.s2_point2_desc: This is where the issue lies. Although competitive for a bank, this rate is still 8 times higher than a Global ETF (~0.17%).
analyseArgenta.s2_point3_desc: Thanks to the absence of entry fees, ARPE shows one of the best historical net returns in its category.
analyseArgenta.s2_warning: ⚠ Even without entry fees, the TER of ~1.44% acts as a permanent drag. Over 30 years, this fee gap with ETFs ends up weighing more heavily than the initial tax benefit.
analyseArgenta.s3_p1: The Belgian system is a tax carrot followed by a stick at exit.
analyseArgenta.s3_list_point1: The carrot: 30% annual tax reduction (9 450 € cumulated over 30 years).
analyseArgenta.s3_list_point2: The stick: 8% tax at age 60 calculated on a notional return of 4.75%.
analyseArgenta.s3_warning: ⚠ Since Argenta ARPE historically returns around 4.5% net, you pay 8% tax on gains you haven't even fully realised (because the tax assumes 4.75%). The State eats a significant share of your final tax benefit.
analyseArgenta.s4_title: The 30-year match: Argenta ARPE vs Global ETF
analyseArgenta.s4_intro: Imagine 1 050 € invested every year for 30 years (total: 31 500 €).
analyseArgenta.s4_key_callout: The ETF generates ~2.5x more net profit (+91 652 € vs +38 715 €)
analyseArgenta.s4_methodology_note: Methodological note: the 8% return used for IMIE is based on the historical average of the MSCI ACWI IMI over 20 years (2005–2025). Over the last 10 years, the actual return was closer to 10–11%/year — the comparison above is therefore favourable to Argenta.
analyseArgenta.s4_cta_text: Compare with your own numbers in our simulator.
analyseArgenta.s5_verdict_p1: Argenta ARPE is genuinely the best pension savings fund in Belgium. The complete absence of entry fees (0%) makes it categorically superior to KBC, BNP, or Belfius offerings. This is a real distinction that deserves recognition.
analyseArgenta.s5_verdict_p2: However, being the best in a mediocre category is still a poor choice for many. For investors under 45, the ETF wins structurally and unambiguously: the TER of ~1.44% over 30 years destroys more value than the tax benefit creates.
analyseArgenta.s5_ideal_desc: Over 50, with a short horizon (10–15 years). The 30% tax bonus doesn't have time to be eroded by fees. Argenta is then the right choice — and the only pension fund worth considering.
analyseArgenta.s5_avoid_title: A note on the tax benefit:
analyseArgenta.s5_avoid_desc: The 30% reduction is fixed and independent of income for employees (max 315 €/year). It never reaches 45% or 50% (reserved for self-employed via other products).
analyseArgenta.tldr_text: Argenta ARPE is Belgium's best pension savings fund: 0% entry fees, competitive management. Yet over 30 years of contributions at the tax maximum (€1,050/year), its ~1.44%/year fees destroy 2.5 times more value than the €315/year tax benefit creates. Net result: +€38,715 vs +€91,652 for a Global ETF — even with the best fund in its category.
analyseArgenta.s3_etf_flex_text: Argenta's 8% tax is unavoidable and calculated in one go at age 60 on a notional return. The ETF capital gains tax (10%) offers real flexibility: by selling gradually in retirement, you benefit each year from the €10 000 exemption. Your effective real rate can fall to 2–3%, making the comparison even more favourable to ETFs than the figures in the next section show.
analyseArgenta.chart_caption: Argenta being the best fund on the market (0% entry), this crossover point arrives later than for KBC or BNP. But it still arrives.
analyseArgenta.s6_title: I've already subscribed to Argenta ARPE — what now?
analyseArgenta.s6_step1_heading: Stop all new contributions to Argenta ARPE.
analyseArgenta.s6_step1_desc: Even without entry fees, every euro contributed today still incurs ~1.44%/year in management fees. The 315 €/year tax benefit does not compensate for this cost over 30 years.
analyseArgenta.s6_step2_heading: Redirect your 1 050 €/year to a Global ETF via a fee-free broker.
analyseArgenta.s6_step2_desc: MeDirect or Saxo Bank (AutoInvest) allow you to invest automatically, with no entry fees, with stock exchange tax handled automatically. Money already at Argenta: leave it until age 60. Withdrawing it early costs more in taxes than you save in fees.
analyseArgenta.s6_summary: Argenta was perhaps the best available choice at the time. But don't put another cent in.
analyseArgenta.s_hybrid_title: The Hybrid Strategy — A Mathematical Myth
analyseArgenta.s_hybrid_intro: This is the most sophisticated argument from pension savings advocates: the 'hybrid' strategy. The logic is appealing: invest 1 050 €/year with Argenta, get back 315 € via taxes, and immediately reinvest those 315 € in a Global ETF. You capture both the tax bonus AND stock market returns. Note: these 315 € are 'free money' that only exists because you chose pension savings. This argument deserves a serious response.
analyseArgenta.s_hybrid_gap_note: Gap: ~29 600 € in favour of the pure ETF strategy.
analyseArgenta.s_hybrid_why_p: The 315 € bonus is flat: it never grows. In contrast, the annual growth gap between the ETF (8%) and Argenta (4.5%) applies to a capital base that itself grows every year. That's the power of compound interest against a fixed bonus.
analyseArgenta.s_hybrid_horizon_title: The only case where hybrid holds up
analyseArgenta.s_hybrid_horizon_desc: For investors aged 50 and over, with a short horizon (10–15 years). The crossover point has not yet been reached and the annual €315 bonus remains higher than the compounding shortfall. This is the only configuration where pension savings makes sense — and Argenta, with 0% entry fees, is the only fund worth considering in this case.
analyseArgenta.s_hybrid_conclusion: The 315 €/year bonus is real. But it is fixed and does not compound. The return gap between a Global ETF and Argenta does compound — and surpasses it around year 8.
analyseArgenta.intro_p1: Argenta sets itself radically apart from its competitors — KBC, Belfius, BNP — with one striking argument: 0% entry fees.
analyseArgenta.intro_p2: Where a classic bank takes 2 to 3% on each contribution, Argenta invests your full amount. On paper, it's the most honest product in the banking sector. Let's see if that's enough.
analyseArgenta.s2_p1: With no entry fees, the full amount of each contribution is invested. Here is nonetheless the stacking of remaining costs:
analyseArgenta.s2_entry_desc: The major advantage: your 1 050 € works in full from day one. No upfront charge, unlike KBC (2%) or BNP (3%).
analyseArgenta.s2_ter_desc: The catch. Although competitive for a bank fund, this TER is still 8 times higher than a Global ETF (~0.17%). Over 30 years, the compounding effect is devastating.
analyseArgenta.s2_effect_desc: The effect over 30 years: on a capital of 66 000 €, 1.44% represents around 924 € in annual fees — every year. Fees that would themselves have compounded at 8% if invested in an ETF.
analyseArgenta.s3_p2: The State calculates this tax on a notional capital, assuming your fund has returned 4.75%/year from day one.
analyseArgenta.s3_p3: ARPE historically returns ~4.5% net — very close to the notional rate. You therefore pay 8% on gains you have almost fully realised. This is the most 'fair' situation among bank funds, but the tax remains a real levy.
analyseArgenta.s3_example_p5: Effective tax rate on your real gains: ~16%
analyseArgenta.s3_p4: Argenta suffers this effect less severely than KBC — but the tax remains unavoidable and is applied to a notional capital you may not have reached.
analyseArgenta.s5_intro: The figures are unequivocal: even with the complete absence of entry fees — Argenta's strongest asset — the annual TER of ~1.44% ends up weighing more heavily than the tax benefit creates over 30 years.
analyseArgenta.s5_point1: 0% entry fees, but ~1.44% TER per year: your capital is fully invested, but the management vehicle takes around 924 € per year on 66 000 € accumulated.
analyseArgenta.s5_point2: The 315 €/year tax benefit is fixed and does not compound — while annual fees accumulate every year on a growing capital base.
analyseArgenta.s5_point3: For those under 45, the effect of annual fees completely destroys the initial tax benefit over the long term — even for the best bank fund.
analyseArgenta.s5_outro: Argenta is Belgium's best pension savings fund. But being the best in a structurally losing category remains a poor deal for most savers.
analyseArgenta.s_caf_title: The Cafeteria Plan — The Only Serious Exception
analyseArgenta.s_caf_intro_p1: If your employer offers a cafeteria plan, you can fund your pension savings with gross euros. This is the only scenario where pension savings becomes mathematically competitive — thanks to a triple simultaneous subsidy: exemption from NSSO contributions (13.07%), gross employer budget, and a 30% tax reduction from the State.
analyseArgenta.s_caf_intro_p2: Let’s see whether this subsidy is sufficient to reverse the conclusion.
analyseArgenta.s_caf_caveat: This analysis applies only to employees whose employer offers a cafeteria plan — mainly large private-sector companies. It assumes a marginal tax rate of 50% (taxable income > ~€46,440/year in 2026). At lower rates, the figures are less favourable for pension savings.
analyseArgenta.s_caf_meca_title: The Mechanics: What Happens to €1,050 Gross?
analyseArgenta.s_caf_meca_explanation: Investor A receives €457 net and invests it in an ETF. Investor B puts €1,050 into pension savings, but their net salary decreases by €210 (€525 BIK tax minus €315 recovered tax reduction).
analyseArgenta.s_caf_compare_title: Same Net Sacrifice: Who Wins Over 30 Years?
analyseArgenta.s_caf_compare_intro: For a fair comparison, we start from the same net sacrifice: €210/year less in the bank account in both cases. Investor A: net bonus of €457 + €210 additional from salary = €667/year in a World ETF. Investor B: cafeteria plan = €1,050/year in ARPE, for a net cost of €210/year.
analyseArgenta.s_caf_callout: Even with the cafeteria plan’s triple advantage — €1,050 invested versus just €667 for the ETF — the World ETF generates ~€9,276 more over 30 years. The ~1.40%/year in annual fees erases the subsidy.
analyseArgenta.s_caf_context_note: Without a cafeteria plan, the gap was ~€53,000 in the ETF’s favour. The cafeteria plan reduces this gap to ~€9,276 — without reversing it. That is significant, but insufficient.
analyseArgenta.s_caf_concl_title: The Cafeteria Plan: Useful, But Not Enough
analyseArgenta.s_caf_concl_p1: The cafeteria plan is real and powerful. It transforms a clearly losing product into something almost competitive. But ‘almost’ is not ‘better’.
analyseArgenta.s_caf_concl_p2: Over 30 years, with the same net sacrifice of €210/year, the World ETF still leads — by ~€9,276. These figures use Argenta ARPE (0% entry fee, the best fund on the market).
analyseArgenta.s_caf_concl_p3: If your employer offers a cafeteria plan and you want to use it for pension savings, Argenta (0% entry fee) remains the only fund worth considering in this context. KBC, BNP, and Belfius make the outcome even worse with their higher entry fees and TERs.
analyseArgenta.s_caf_concl_p4: But the conclusion is the same with or without a cafeteria plan: over a long horizon, the World ETF produces more net wealth for the same financial effort.
analyseArgenta.s_caf_methodology: Methodology note: calculations based on a 50% marginal tax rate (taxable income > ~€46,440/year in 2026) and an employee NSSO rate of 13.07%. BIK taxed at the marginal rate. ARPE return: 4.5% net (Argenta — best fund on the market). ETF return: 8%/year (historical average MSCI ACWI IMI 2005–2025). CGT calculated on a single sale — in practice, progressive selling reduces the ETF CGT to near zero, making the ETF even more advantageous.
analyseArgenta.s_caf_disclaimer: Indicative simulation. Past performance is not a guarantee of future returns.

analyseBnpPension.s2_ter_desc: The silent killer. These fees apply every year to your entire accumulated capital. Although slightly lower than KBC (1.53%), the impact over 30 years remains considerable.

analyseBnpPension.s3_p2: The State calculates it on a notional capital, assuming your fund has returned 4.75% per year from day one.
analyseBnpPension.s3_p3: The problem: BNP B Pension rarely reaches 4.75% net of fees. You therefore pay 8% tax on gains you may never have actually realised.
analyseBnpPension.s3_example_p1: 30 years of contributions at 1 050 €/year (1 018.50 € effective after 3% entry fee).
analyseBnpPension.s3_example_p5: Effective tax rate on your real gains: ~18%
analyseBnpPension.s3_p4: The more your fund underperforms relative to the notional rate of 4.75%, the more punitive your effective tax rate. BNP combines high entry fees WITH an exit tax on notional gains.
analyseBnpPension.s3_etf_flex_text: Unlike BNP's 8% tax (taken in one shot at 60 on a notional capital), the ETF capital gains tax of 10% offers real flexibility: by selling gradually in retirement, you benefit each year from the 10 000 € exemption. In practice, your effective real rate can fall to 2–3%.

analyseBnpPension.s4_note: The Global ETF generates nearly 3 times more real profit — even after the new 2026 capital gains tax and without a tax benefit.
analyseBnpPension.s4_methodology_note: * Methodological note: BNP B Pension's 4.3% return is based on its last 10 years. Over that same period, IMIE actually delivered ~10–11%/year. We use 8% for consistency with our long-term projections. This comparison favours BNP.

analyseBnpPension.s_hybrid_conclusion: The 315 €/year bonus is real. But 3% entry fees reduce it to 283.50 € net. And this shrinking advantage is overtaken at year 5 by the compounded return gap.

analyseBnpPension.s5_intro: The numbers are clear: BNP B Pension is the most expensive pension savings fund among Belgium's major players, with the highest entry fees on the market.

analyseBnpPension.s5_point2: The 315 €/year tax benefit is a fixed bonus that does not compound — while annual fees accumulate every year on a growing capital base.
analyseBnpPension.s5_point3: The ESG label creates no extra return. Over 30 years, BNP generates less value than a standard ESG ETF at 0.20% annual fees.
analyseBnpPension.s5_outro: If you want to invest responsibly, an ESG ETF (MSCI World SRI, iShares MSCI EM SRI) costs you 15 times less annually — and without the 3% entry fee.

ethiasAnalysis.hero_subtitle: 2.90% guaranteed over 9 years. Before you sign, here is what the brochure doesn't say — and why there is a better option for every type of investor.
ethiasAnalysis.tldr_text: Ethias Savings 21+ advertises 2.90% guaranteed — but the 2% premium tax deducted on day one reduces your real return to ~2.74% on the initial capital. Over 9 years, XEON (money market ETF) delivers the same return with no entry cost, no lock-in, and no €20,000 minimum. Over the long term, a World ETF does nearly 3 times better. Branch 21 has no use case that an ETF alternative doesn't cover more effectively.
ethiasAnalysis.s1_title: The Structural Problem: The 2% Premium Tax
ethiasAnalysis.s1_p1: Every Branch 21 product in Belgium — Ethias, AG Insurance, Belfius, AXA — shares the same structural flaw: a 2% premium tax on every payment. This is a legislative tax. No insurer can waive or negotiate it.
ethiasAnalysis.s1_p2: It is deducted before your money is invested. On a €20,000 payment, €400 disappears immediately and permanently — before a single euro is put to work.
ethiasAnalysis.s1_p3: At 2.90% gross per year, it takes approximately 8.4 months just to recover the day-one loss before generating any real return.
ethiasAnalysis.s1_val_duration: 9 years (early exit → 30% withholding tax on notional 4.75%/yr return)
ethiasAnalysis.s1_warning: The 2% premium tax is not a management fee recoverable over time. It is money lost on day one, on every payment, permanently. No Branch 21 product in Belgium avoids it.
ethiasAnalysis.s2_title: For a Short Horizon: XEON Wins on Every Dimension
ethiasAnalysis.s2_intro: Branch 21 targets savers who want to 'secure' their money over the short to medium term. XEON — a money market ETF tracking the ECB's €STR rate — covers exactly that need, without any of Branch 21's structural drawbacks.
ethiasAnalysis.s2_callout: Over 9 years on €20,000, the World ETF generates ~€38,910 net — €14,000 more than Ethias 21+. Even XEON, with no market risk, does better (+€25,900 vs +€24,900).
ethiasAnalysis.s2_objection_p: XEON tracks the European overnight interbank rate (€STR). It is not contractually guaranteed. But XEON has never recorded a loss over any 12-month period since inception. The contractual guarantee Ethias offers costs 2% upfront, a 9-year lock-in, and a €20,000 minimum. That is a very expensive insurance premium against a risk that has never materialised historically.
ethiasAnalysis.s2_caveat: One genuine advantage exists: if ECB rates drop significantly, XEON's return decreases with them. Ethias's 2.90% is locked for 9 years. That is the only concrete advantage of Ethias Savings 21+ — and it comes at a considerable structural cost.
ethiasAnalysis.s2_warning: If stability is your goal, XEON delivers the same return as Ethias 21+ with no entry costs, no lock-in, and no €20,000 minimum. The only thing Ethias is selling you is a contractual guarantee against a rate drop — at the cost of 2% of your capital, today.
ethiasAnalysis.s3_intro: Over 30 years, choosing Ethias Savings 21+ — or any Branch 21 product — instead of a World ETF represents an enormous opportunity cost.
ethiasAnalysis.s3_table_row1_val2: −0.12% securities tax at buy and sell
ethiasAnalysis.s3_callout: Even after fully crediting the CGT exemption, the World ETF generates ~4.8× more than Ethias 21+ over 30 years — a gap of ~€145,000.
ethiasAnalysis.s3_methodology: Methodology note: simulation based on a lump-sum investment of €20,000. The 8% return for IMIE is based on the historical average of the MSCI World index over 30 years. Past performance does not guarantee future results.
ethiasAnalysis.s4_verdict_1: Branch 21 is structurally disadvantaged by the 2% premium tax — a legislative cost that applies to every provider without exception. This is not an Ethias-specific problem: it is a categorical flaw.
ethiasAnalysis.s4_verdict_2: For a short horizon, XEON is strictly better on every dimension except the contractual rate lock. That lock costs 2% upfront, a 9-year lock-in, and a €20,000 minimum. For the vast majority of savers, that is not a favourable trade.
ethiasAnalysis.s4_verdict_3: Over 30 years, a World ETF generates ~4.8× more net value than Ethias Savings 21+ — a gap of ~€145,000 after taxes.
ethiasAnalysis.s4_verdict_4: Within its category, Ethias is not the worst Branch 21 product — 0% management fees and direct distribution (no intermediary commission) are genuine advantages over AG Insurance or Belfius. But being the best in a structurally bad category is still a bad choice.
ethiasAnalysis.s4_verdict_5: There is no profile of Belgian investor for whom Ethias Savings 21+ is the optimal choice. Short horizon: XEON. Long horizon: World ETF.
ethiasAnalysis.cta_secondary: Discover the XEON Liquid Savings strategy
ethiasAnalysis.disclaimer: Illustrative simulation only. Past returns do not guarantee future returns. This content is provided for educational purposes only and does not constitute investment advice within the meaning of MiFID II regulation.
ethiasAnalysis.s2_bridge: Over 9 years — the duration the product itself proposes — the World ETF already wins. Over 30 years, the gap becomes a life decision.

analyseAgSafePlus.meta_title: AG Safe+ Review — €213,000 in Lost Profit for ‘Safety’
analyseAgSafePlus.meta_description: Full review of AG Safe+ (Branch 21, AG Insurance). 2% premium tax, ~2.80%/year return. Over 30 years, the gap with a World ETF exceeds €213,000 even after full credit for the CGT exemption.
analyseAgSafePlus.hero_subtitle: Guaranteed capital, guaranteed rate, zero market risk. AG Insurance sells peace of mind. Here is what that peace of mind actually costs you over 30 years.
analyseAgSafePlus.tldr_text: AG Safe+ combines 2% premium tax + ~1% entry fee = 3% total friction before investment. Guaranteed rate 2.75% (year 1) / 2.25% (subsequent years) + unguaranteed profit participation → estimated net return ~2.80%/year. Branch 21 is exempt from capital gains tax — a genuine tax advantage, already fully credited in our simulation. It represents ~€5,720 over 30 years. The gap with a World ETF remains ~€213,000. There is no investor profile for whom AG Safe+ is the optimal choice over a long horizon.
analyseAgSafePlus.s1_val_net_return: ~2.80%/year estimated (2.75% guaranteed year 1, 2.25% guaranteed thereafter + ~1.00% non-guaranteed profit sharing)
analyseAgSafePlus.s1_source: Rates and fees based on AG Insurance product documentation. Guaranteed rate and profit participation subject to change. Verify current conditions before subscribing.
analyseAgSafePlus.s2_title: The Premium Tax — A Statutory Levy on Every Euro Deposited
analyseAgSafePlus.s2_p1: The 2% premium tax is a statutory requirement (Art. 173 ITC) that applies to every payment in any Belgian Branch 21 life insurance product. No provider can waive it. Switching broker or contract does not remove it.
analyseAgSafePlus.s2_p2: In practice: of €300 deposited per month, €6 disappears immediately in premium tax before any investment. Over 30 years, that is €2,160 lost to premium tax alone.
analyseAgSafePlus.s2_p3: Combined with ~1% entry fees, only €291 of your €300 actually starts working. And at ~2.80%/year, it takes roughly 13 months of guaranteed return just to recover your initial investment.
analyseAgSafePlus.s2_recovery_callout: At ~2.80% net/year, it takes ~13 months of guaranteed return just to recover your initial outlay after the 3% entry friction. In that time, a World ETF has already gained ~2%.
analyseAgSafePlus.s2_warning: The 2% premium tax is not a negotiable fee — it is a statutory levy (Art. 173 ITC). It applies to every payment, forever, regardless of the contract’s return.
analyseAgSafePlus.s3_point_a_title: Withholding tax — No advantage versus an accumulating ETF
analyseAgSafePlus.s3_point_a_p1: Branch 21 is exempt from withholding tax on interest. That is true. But accumulating ETFs like IMIE distribute no dividends and therefore also pay 0% withholding tax.
analyseAgSafePlus.s3_point_a_p2: You pay 3% entry fees to ‘buy’ a withholding tax exemption that the accumulating ETF already gives you for free. This is the equivalent of buying insurance against a risk you don’t have.
analyseAgSafePlus.s3_callout_a_label: WITHHOLDING TAX — NO ADVANTAGE VS ETF
analyseAgSafePlus.s3_callout_a: Branch 21’s withholding tax exemption gives you no advantage over an accumulating ETF like IMIE. Both pay 0% withholding tax. You pay 3% upfront for protection against a problem you don’t have.
analyseAgSafePlus.s3_point_b_title: Capital gains tax — Genuine advantage, already credited in the simulation
analyseAgSafePlus.s3_point_b_p1: Branch 21 is exempt from the 10% capital gains tax (CGT). ETFs are not — they pay 10% on gains above €10,000/year. This is a genuine and documented tax advantage.
analyseAgSafePlus.s3_point_b_p2: We fully credit this in our simulation: €0 CGT for AG Safe+, versus €29,000 for the ETF. This advantage represents ~€5,720 over 30 years — less than 3% of the total gap of ~€213,000.
analyseAgSafePlus.s3_callout_b: Branch 21 is exempt from capital gains tax (10%). ETFs are not — they pay 10% on gains above €10,000/year. This advantage is credited in our simulation (€0 CGT for AG Safe+). It represents ~€5,720 over 30 years — less than 3% of the total gap of ~€213,000.
analyseAgSafePlus.s4_title: 9-Year Horizon — Even Over the Contract’s Own Duration
analyseAgSafePlus.s4_intro: Simulation on a lump-sum investment of €20,000, horizon 9 years — the maturity proposed by Branch 21 products. All amounts are net values (after fees and taxes).
analyseAgSafePlus.s4_callout: Over 9 years, XEON beats AG Safe+ (+€25,900 vs +€24,800) with full liquidity and 0% management fees. The World ETF goes further still (+€38,910 net).
analyseAgSafePlus.s4_methodology_note: Methodology note: the 8% return used for IMIE is based on the historical average of the MSCI ACWI IMI over 20 years (2005–2025). The 2.80% return for AG Safe+ is optimistic: it includes unguaranteed profit participation. Branch 21’s CGT exemption is fully credited to AG Safe+ in this simulation.
analyseAgSafePlus.s4_cta_text: Simulate your own situation with your amount and time horizon.
analyseAgSafePlus.s5_p1: The real cost of “security” shows up over the long term. Over 30 years, successive renewals of AG Safe+ leave an enormous gap versus a World ETF.
analyseAgSafePlus.s5_objection_p: XEON replicates the €STR rate, the ECB’s overnight interbank rate. It has never lost value over any 12-month period since inception. AG Safe+’s contractual guarantee costs you 3% entry + an 8-year lock-in — a very expensive insurance premium against a risk that has never materialised.
analyseAgSafePlus.s5_caveat: The one genuine difference in AG Safe+’s favour: if the ECB cuts rates significantly, XEON follows down. AG Safe+’s guaranteed rate of 2.75% (year 1) / 2.25% (subsequent years) is locked in for the contract duration. This is the only genuine advantage AG Safe+ has over XEON — and it comes with a 3% structural cost and an 8-year lock-in.
analyseAgSafePlus.s5_warning: If stability is your objective over a short horizon, XEON offers a similar return to AG Safe+ with no entry fees, no lock-in, and no minimum. The only thing AG Safe+ is selling you on this front is a rate guarantee against a rate cut — at the cost of 3% of your capital, starting today.
analyseAgSafePlus.s5_xref: For a detailed Branch 21 vs XEON comparison on a short horizon, see also our Ethias Savings 21+ review.
analyseAgSafePlus.s6_verdict_1: AG Safe+ is a legally sound product — guaranteed capital, regulated insurer, clear contract. We do not question the solidity of AG Insurance.
analyseAgSafePlus.s6_verdict_2: But Branch 21 carries an unavoidable structural cost: the 2% premium tax on every payment, combined with a return capped at ~2.80%/year. Over 30 years, this cost far exceeds the CGT advantage Branch 21 offers over ETFs.
analyseAgSafePlus.s6_verdict_3: Over 30 years, the World ETF IMIE generates ~4.1× more than AG Safe+ — a gap of ~€139,000 after taxes. The 3% entry friction is structurally disadvantageous for long-term savers.
analyseAgSafePlus.s6_verdict_4: Over a short horizon (< 8 years): XEON offers a comparable return, with no entry fees, no lock-in, and no minimum.
analyseAgSafePlus.s6_verdict_5: There is no investor profile for whom AG Safe+ is the optimal choice over a long horizon. For any horizon: an ETF alternative does better.
analyseAgSafePlus.s6_case_legit_desc: None that withstands comparison. For a long horizon: World ETF. For a short horizon: XEON. For estate planning: AG Fund+ (Branch 23) offers beneficiary designation with greater return flexibility.
analyseAgSafePlus.s6_avoid_desc: Any investor with a horizon over 3 years who has access to an ETF broker. Note: ‘I can’t tolerate volatility’ is not a sufficient reason — a World ETF has never produced a real loss over 15 years. Short-term volatility is not a long-term loss risk.
analyseAgSafePlus.disclaimer: Illustrative simulation only. Past returns do not guarantee future returns. This tool does not constitute investment advice under the MiFID II directive.
analyseAgSafePlus.s4_bridge: Over 9 years — the duration the product itself proposes — the World ETF already wins. Over 30 years, the gap becomes a life decision.
analyseAgSafePlus.s5_callout: The World ETF generates ~4.1× more than AG Safe+ over 30 years — a gap of ~€139,000, even after crediting Branch 21’s CGT exemption.
analyseAgSafePlus.s5_methodology: Methodology note: simulation based on a lump-sum investment of €20,000. For the 30-year horizon, successive renewals of the Branch 21 product are assumed (entry fees re-applied at each maturity). The 8% return for IMIE is based on the historical average of the MSCI World index over 30 years. Past performance does not guarantee future results.

analyseBaloiseInvest.meta_title: Baloise Invest 23 Review — Up to 5.5% Entry Costs
analyseBaloiseInvest.meta_description: Full review of Baloise Invest 23 Branch 23. The most expensive Branch 23 product on the market: up to 5.50% total entry friction and ~2.20%–2.50%/year in fees.
analyseBaloiseInvest.hero_title: Baloise Invest 23 — The Most Expensive Branch 23 Product on the Market
analyseBaloiseInvest.hero_subtitle: Up to 5.50% entry friction, ~2.20% to 2.50%/year in fees, and no capital guarantee. for a tax treatment identical to a direct ETF.
analyseBaloiseInvest.tldr_text: Baloise Invest 23 combines up to 5.50% entry friction (2% premium tax + up to 3.50% entry fees) and ~2.20% to 2.50%/year in total costs. The product offers no capital guarantee — but charges 1% to 3% if you exit within the first 3 years. Branch 23 has never offered a real tax advantage over an accumulator ETF like IMIE. Over 30 years at €300/month, the gap versus a World ETF exceeds €184,000 — using deliberately conservative assumptions.
analyseBaloiseInvest.s1_val_cgt: 10% on gains (> €10,000/year) — exempt under long-term savings scheme (conditions apply)
analyseBaloiseInvest.s1_source: Source: KID Baloise Invest 23 (BLSBLG000005 / BLSBLG000004), FSMA, 25 March 2026.
analyseBaloiseInvest.s2_title: Branch 23 and Accumulator ETFs: Identical Tax Treatment
analyseBaloiseInvest.s2_p1: The sales pitch for Branch 23 rests on a promise: exemption from withholding tax and capital gains tax.
analyseBaloiseInvest.s2_p2: That promise is misleading for investors in accumulator ETFs like IMIE. Since these ETFs pay no dividends, there is no withholding tax to avoid — not before 2026, not after. The 10% capital gains tax applies identically to both solutions.
analyseBaloiseInvest.s2_p3: Branch 23 has never offered a real tax advantage over IMIE. The advantage only existed versus distributing ETFs (30% withholding tax on dividends), bond funds subject to the Reynders tax, or savings accounts — distinct product categories. Baloise was selling protection against a tax problem that accumulator ETF investors never had — at ~2.20%/year in fees.
analyseBaloiseInvest.s2_warning: Branch 23 has never offered a real tax advantage over an accumulator ETF like IMIE — not before 2026, not after. What Baloise Invest 23 sells you is the illusion of tax protection, at the cost of ~2.20%/year in extra fees.
analyseBaloiseInvest.s3_point1_desc: Identical for all Branch 23 products in Belgium — statutory, no insurer can avoid it. At €300/month, €6 disappears before being invested. Over 30 years, this amounts to €2,160 lost in premium tax alone.
analyseBaloiseInvest.s3_point2_title: Entry fees: up to 3.50% — The Broker's Commission
analyseBaloiseInvest.s3_point2_desc: Official maximum rate: 3.50%. Some brokers offer a discount — minimum observed: ~2.50%. Our simulation uses 3.00% (midpoint of the range, favourable to Baloise). Total entry friction: up to 5.50%. On €10,000: up to €550 disappears before investment — roughly 11 months of returns at 5.80% net just to recover the initial amount.
analyseBaloiseInvest.s3_point3_title: Double layer of annual fees: ~2.20% to 2.50%
analyseBaloiseInvest.s3_point3_desc: Baloise wrapper fees: ~1.10% to 1.70%/year. TER of underlying funds (BlackRock, Pictet, internal Baloise funds): ~0.40% to 1.20%/year. Total: ~2.20% to 2.50%/year for an equity profile. On a capital of €100,000: up to €2,500/year in fees, versus €170/year for a World ETF at 0.17% TER. Baloise Invest 23 costs up to 14 times more than a direct ETF.
analyseBaloiseInvest.s3_point4_desc: First period of 1,000 days (~3 years): Year 1: −3% / Year 2: −2% / Year 3: −1% / Year 4+: 0%. On a capital of €10,000: an exit in year 1 costs an extra €300 — on top of entry and management fees already charged. The product offers no capital guarantee, but penalises you for wanting to exit.
analyseBaloiseInvest.s3_warning: Baloise Invest 23 offers no capital guarantee — but penalises you for exiting within the first 3 years. You bear market risk without the liquidity of an ETF, and pay up to 14 times more in annual fees for that privilege.
analyseBaloiseInvest.s3_broker_note: Baloise distributes primarily through independent brokers. In theory, an independent broker should recommend the most suitable product. In practice, entry fees (up to 3.50%) and management fee retrocessions create a structural bias: the products most rewarding for the broker are rarely the cheapest for the investor. Baloise's underlying funds include BlackRock and Pictet products that you could buy directly for a tenth of the total annual cost.
analyseBaloiseInvest.s4_intro: Comparison based on €300/month (€3,600/year) over 30 years. Total invested: €108,000. Same basis as the NN Strategy and AG Fund+ analyses for direct comparability. Assumptions: 3.00% entry fees (midpoint of confirmed range) and 2.00%/year total fees (conservative vs. the real range of 2.20%–2.50%) — deliberately favourable to Baloise.
analyseBaloiseInvest.s4_callout: The World ETF generates ~3.2x more net gain (+€270,611 vs +€85,860) — a gap of ~€184,750.
analyseBaloiseInvest.s4_b23_intro: Within the universe of Branch 23 products reviewed on ClearInvest (same basis: €300/month, 30 years):
analyseBaloiseInvest.s4_b23_callout: All three Branch 23 products lose massively to the ETF (+€270,611). Baloise is the most expensive and least favourable to the investor of the three.
analyseBaloiseInvest.s4_nuance_note: Note: the ETF simulation applies capital gains tax as if the entire capital were sold at once at the end. In practice, an ETF investor can reduce the actual tax to near zero by selling progressively (in annual tranches of €10,000 in gains). The real gap is therefore even more favourable to the ETF.
analyseBaloiseInvest.s4_methodology_note: Methodological note: the 8% return used for IMIE is based on the 20-year historical average of the MSCI ACWI IMI (2005–2025). The 2.00%/year fees used for Baloise Invest 23 are conservative: the real range is 2.20% to 2.50% for an equity profile. The 3.00% entry fee represents the midpoint of the official range (up to 3.50%). The comparison is therefore deliberately favourable to Baloise.
analyseBaloiseInvest.s4_cta_text: Run the numbers with your own figures in our calculator.
analyseBaloiseInvest.s5_p1: A Branch 23 contract allows you to designate specific beneficiaries who receive the capital directly upon death, bypassing the standard Belgian succession procedure. Baloise Insurance Belgium is a solid, regulated insurer — the beneficiary designation is legally robust. For investors over 60 who have already built up wealth and wish to plan its transfer, this legal advantage has real value.
analyseBaloiseInvest.s5_p2: This estate planning need does not justify paying ~2.20% in fees per year for 30 years during the wealth-building phase. It is a transfer instrument, not an investment vehicle.
analyseBaloiseInvest.s5_callout: Branch 23 remains a legitimate estate planning tool for built-up wealth. For the building phase, the fees of Baloise Invest 23 represent a cost with no counterpart. Consult a notary before subscribing.
analyseBaloiseInvest.s6_verdict_p1: Baloise Invest 23 is the most expensive Branch 23 product reviewed on ClearInvest. Up to 5.50% entry friction and ~2.20% to 2.50%/year in annual fees.
analyseBaloiseInvest.s6_verdict_p2: The product offers no capital guarantee — but locks you in for 3 years with declining exit fees. You bear market risk without the liquidity of an ETF, at up to 14 times the annual cost of a World ETF.
analyseBaloiseInvest.s6_verdict_p3: Branch 23 has never offered a tax advantage over an accumulator ETF like IMIE — neither before nor after 2026. The tax argument was an illusion sold to investors who were not comparing against the right reference product.
analyseBaloiseInvest.s6_verdict_p4: Distribution through 'independent' brokers: retrocessions on entry and management fees create a structural bias towards Baloise — not the investor.
analyseBaloiseInvest.s6_verdict_p5: Estate planning: the only legitimate use case, exclusively for investors with built-up wealth in a transfer phase. For any investor in the wealth-building phase: avoid.
analyseBaloiseInvest.s6_ideal_desc: Estate planning, investors 60+, built-up wealth, targeted transfer outside standard succession. In that case: consult a notary before subscribing.
analyseBaloiseInvest.s6_avoid_desc: Any investor seeking to build wealth.
analyseBaloiseInvest.s6_exit_note: If you subscribed recently (less than 3 years ago), exit fees of 1% to 3% apply. Calculate the exit cost versus the cost of staying before deciding. After 3 years, switching to ETFs is mathematically justified in the vast majority of cases.
analyseBaloiseInvest.disclaimer: For illustrative purposes only. Past performance does not guarantee future returns.
analyseBaloiseInvest.s5_lts_p1: Under the long-term savings scheme, Branch 23 contracts can benefit from a 30% tax reduction and a capital gains tax exemption from 2026, subject to access conditions for the federal tax basket.
analyseBaloiseInvest.s5_lts_p2: Does this change the verdict for Baloise Invest 23?
analyseBaloiseInvest.s5_lts_p3: No — and even less so than for its competitors.
analyseBaloiseInvest.s5_lts_p4: With up to 5.5% entry friction and 2.2% to 2.5%/year in total annual fees, Baloise is the Branch 23 product furthest from the conditions required for long-term savings to be competitive with an ETF. For comparison, the viability threshold is around ~3% entry friction and ~1.4%/year in fees — Baloise exceeds both thresholds significantly.
analyseBaloiseInvest.s5_lts_p5: Furthermore, Baloise’s underlying funds are actively managed, which adds a gross return deficit on top of the fees. Long-term savings is a genuine opportunity for the right products; Baloise Invest 23 is not one of them.

analyseAxaIndex4p.meta_title: AXA Index4P Global Equity: Full Review — Branch 23 and Long-Term Savings 2026
analyseAxaIndex4p.meta_description: AXA Index4P is the only Belgian Branch 23 product with passive management and 1.40%/year fees. Under long-term savings conditions, it can outperform a World ETF — full analysis and conditions.
analyseAxaIndex4p.hero_title: AXA Index4P Global Equity — the only case where Branch 23 can compete with an ETF
analyseAxaIndex4p.hero_subtitle: Passive management, 1.40%/year fees. Under the long-term savings scheme, this is the only Branch 23 product in our review where the math can favour the insurance wrapper — under strict conditions.
analyseAxaIndex4p.tldr_text: AXA Index4P stands apart in the Belgian Branch 23 universe: passive management on a global index, 1.40%/year fees — the lowest on the market. Under long-term savings conditions, 30-year simulations show a result competitive with a World ETF: slightly ahead in a lump-sale scenario, roughly equivalent with progressive withdrawals. It is the only Branch 23 product in our analysis where this holds — and only under three conditions: access to the federal tax basket, entry fees ≤ 1%, and tax refund reinvested every year.
analyseAxaIndex4p.s1_val_underlying: Amundi MSCI World Climate Transition IE (C)
analyseAxaIndex4p.s1_val_cgt: 10% (€10,000/year exemption) — exempt under long-term savings scheme
analyseAxaIndex4p.s1_source: Source: AXA Index4P Global Equity fund sheet, AXA Belgium S.A., April 2026. Fund launched on 13/10/2025 — the performance figures shown are those of the underlying fund (Amundi MSCI World Climate Transition), not of the AXA contract itself.
analyseAxaIndex4p.s2_title: What sets AXA Index4P apart from other Branch 23 products
analyseAxaIndex4p.s2_p1: Almost all Branch 23 products distributed in Belgium are actively managed funds: managers select securities hoping to beat the market. In practice, between 85% and 92% of these funds underperform their benchmark index over 20 years — before even accounting for the insurance wrapper fees.
analyseAxaIndex4p.s2_p2: AXA Index4P is an exception: the underlying fund (Amundi MSCI World Climate Transition) passively tracks a world index. This means the product’s gross return is close to the global market return — unlike NN Strategy, AG Fund+, or Baloise, whose gross return already trails the market before deducting wrapper fees.
analyseAxaIndex4p.s2_p3: This difference — passive vs. active management — is what changes the calculation for the long-term savings scheme.
analyseAxaIndex4p.s2_index_note: Note: the fund tracks the Amundi MSCI World Climate Transition index, an ESG-filtered version of the MSCI World (exclusion of high carbon-intensity companies). The composition and historical performance are very close to the standard MSCI World — geographic and sector exposure is nearly identical.
analyseAxaIndex4p.s3_point1_desc: Deducted from every contribution — identical for all Branch 23 products in Belgium. Non-negotiable.
analyseAxaIndex4p.s3_point2_desc: The official fund sheet does not mention commercial entry fees separate from the premium tax. Depending on the distributor (broker or AXA directly), additional fees may apply. Negotiate this point before subscribing — it is the most impactful lever on final performance. Our simulation uses 1% (cautious scenario) and 0% (best case).
analyseAxaIndex4p.s3_point3_desc: This is the lowest fee of all Branch 23 products analysed on ClearInvest. For comparison: NN Strategy ~1.50%, AG Fund+ ~1.70%, Baloise ~2.20-2.50%. These 1.40% are nevertheless 8 times higher than the IMIE ETF at 0.17%/year.
analyseAxaIndex4p.s3_warning: ⚠️ The exit fees of 3.6% (decreasing over ~36 months) represent a real liquidity risk. If you need to recover your capital within the first 3 years, the penalty may exceed the accumulated tax benefit. Only subscribe if you are certain of your long-term time horizon.
analyseAxaIndex4p.s4_title: Long-term savings — the only framework where AXA can beat an ETF
analyseAxaIndex4p.s4_intro: In the standard framework (free investment, no tax benefit), AXA Index4P loses to a World ETF — the 1.40%/year fees create a significant cumulative performance gap over 30 years. The situation changes under the long-term savings scheme.
analyseAxaIndex4p.s4_lts_p1: The long-term savings scheme (langetermijnsparen) is a federal tax system that allows deducting 30% of premiums paid into a Branch 21 or 23 life insurance contract (up to €2,450/year, i.e. up to €735 in annual tax rebate). The capital is also exempt from the 2026 capital gains tax — but subject to a 10% exit tax at age 60.
analyseAxaIndex4p.s4_lts_p2: This scheme is only accessible to taxpayers whose federal tax basket is available — mainly those whose mortgage was taken out after 2016 (Flanders/Wallonia) or 2017 (Brussels), or who have no mortgage.
analyseAxaIndex4p.s4_lts_link_text: → Read the full explanation in our banking products guide
analyseAxaIndex4p.s4_match_title: The 30-year match: same net sacrifice of €1,715/year
analyseAxaIndex4p.s4_match_explain: For a fair comparison, we assume both investors have the same annual net sacrifice of €1,715: the ETF investor puts €1,715 directly into IMIE; the AXA investor contributes €2,450 to the contract and receives a €735 tax rebate, which is reinvested. Gross return used: 8%/year (20-year historical average of the MSCI ACWI IMI, 2005-2025).
analyseAxaIndex4p.s4_conditions_note:
In the lump-sale scenario (full liquidation at the end of the period), AXA Index4P leads the ETF by ~€15,000 to €17,000 thanks to the combination of tax benefit and CGT exemption.

In the progressive sale scenario (annual withdrawals at retirement using the €10,000/year CGT exemption), both options arrive at a roughly equivalent result. Full use of the exemption takes approximately 20 years of disciplined withdrawals — realistic but not guaranteed.

Conclusion: AXA Index4P is competitive with an ETF under long-term savings conditions — not clearly superior, but not inferior either. It is one of the only Branch 23 products for which this is true.
analyseAxaIndex4p.s4_methodology_note: * Methodology note: 8%/year gross return based on the 20-year historical average of MSCI ACWI IMI (2005–2025), applied identically to both products. AXA fees: 1.40%/year (confirmed from official fund sheet) + 3% total entry friction (2% statutory premium tax + 1% estimated commercial entry fee — negotiable depending on distributor). The €735/year tax refund is assumed reinvested in an ETF side-pot, not spent. If the refund is not reinvested, the ETF wins in all scenarios. ETF CGT in progressive sale scenario is estimated over 20 years of annual withdrawals of ~€10,000 in gains — realistic for a retiree with an established ETF portfolio.
analyseAxaIndex4p.s4_cta_text: Run the numbers with your own figures in our calculator.
analyseAxaIndex4p.s5_title: Without the long-term savings scheme, the ETF wins
analyseAxaIndex4p.s5_p1: If you do not have access to the federal tax basket — older mortgage, or simply not interested in the scheme — AXA Index4P reverts to a product that underperforms a direct ETF.
analyseAxaIndex4p.s5_callout: Over 30 years with an equivalent contribution of €1,715/year and no tax benefit: IMIE ETF ~€104,200 net — AXA Index4P ~€89,500 net — Gap: −€14,700 for AXA. The 1.40%/year fees create a significant cumulative gap even with passive management — without the tax lever, the insurance wrapper has no mathematical justification.
analyseAxaIndex4p.s6_case1_desc: Taxpayer with access to the federal tax basket (recent mortgage or no mortgage), time horizon ≥ 20 years, ability to negotiate entry fees to ≤ 1% and to reinvest the annual tax rebate. In this specific case, AXA Index4P is the best Branch 23 product available on the Belgian market.
analyseAxaIndex4p.s6_case2_desc: Like any Branch 23 contract, AXA Index4P allows naming specific beneficiaries who receive the capital directly upon death, outside the standard inheritance process. A legitimate case for investors aged 60+ with an established portfolio.
analyseAxaIndex4p.s7_verdict_p1: AXA Index4P Global Equity is a positive anomaly in the Belgian Branch 23 universe: passive management on a world index, fees among the lowest on the market.
analyseAxaIndex4p.s7_verdict_p2: Under the long-term savings scheme and with strict conditions met (access to the tax basket, entry fees negotiated to ≤ 1%, tax rebate reinvested), AXA Index4P is competitive with a World ETF over 30 years — slightly superior in a lump-sale scenario (~+€15,000 to €17,000), roughly equivalent with a progressive withdrawal strategy at retirement. This is the only Branch 23 alternative that is mathematically justifiable for wealth accumulation.
analyseAxaIndex4p.s7_verdict_p3: Without these conditions, the ETF remains superior. And even in the best case, the 3.6% exit fees (decreasing over 36 months) and capital lock-up until age 60 remain real constraints to factor in.
analyseAxaIndex4p.s7_ideal_desc: Taxpayers with an available federal tax basket, time horizon ≥ 20 years, discipline to reinvest the annual tax rebate.
analyseAxaIndex4p.s7_avoid_desc: No access to the federal tax basket, time horizon < 10 years, or uncertainty about the ability to sustain contributions over the long term.

analyseViviumSelection.meta_title: Vivium Selection Dynamic plus Passive: Full Review — 2.54%/year for ETFs You Could Buy Directly
analyseViviumSelection.meta_description: Vivium Selection is a Branch 23 contract invested in ETFs but charged at 2.54%/year. Full cost analysis, ETF comparison, and verdict on this €25,000 minimum product.
analyseViviumSelection.hero_title: Vivium Selection Dynamic plus Passive — 2.54%/year to track ETFs you could buy directly
analyseViviumSelection.hero_subtitle: The product uses ETFs as underlying assets — but the insurance wrapper adds 2.37%/year in fees. Over 10 years, that's tens of thousands of euros lost compared to investing directly.
analyseViviumSelection.tldr_text: Vivium Selection Dynamic plus Passive is a Branch 23 contract that invests in ETFs — but through an insurance wrapper charged at 2.54%/year (RIY). With a €25,000 minimum, the product targets investors with significant capital. Over 10 years (recommended horizon), the total cost of the wrapper represents 2.54% × accumulated capital — tens of thousands of euros you would have kept by buying the same ETFs directly.
analyseViviumSelection.s1_val_underlying: ETFs and index funds (98% equities, 2% cash)
analyseViviumSelection.s1_val_exit: €75 flat (first redemption/year free, no % exit fee after recommended period)
analyseViviumSelection.s1_val_lts: Not compatible — €25,000 minimum incompatible with €2,450/year ceiling
analyseViviumSelection.s1_source: Source: Key Information Document (KID) for Vivium Selection Dynamic plus Passive, P&V Verzekeringen CV, 01/08/2024. Fees shown are from the official KID available on the FSMA website.
analyseViviumSelection.s2_title: ETFs inside a wrapper that costs more than the ETFs themselves
analyseViviumSelection.s2_p1: Vivium Selection Dynamic plus Passive has one feature that sets it apart from most Branch 23 products: it invests in ETFs and index funds as underlying assets (98% equities). On paper, this sounds attractive.
analyseViviumSelection.s2_p2: The problem: the insurance wrapper itself costs 2.23%/year in management and administration fees, plus 0.02% in transaction fees — totalling 2.54%/year in annual cost (RIY).
analyseViviumSelection.s2_p3: For comparison: a global ETF like IMIE costs 0.17%/year. Vivium therefore charges you 2.37%/year extra to access ETFs you could buy directly through any Belgian broker.
analyseViviumSelection.s2_warning: ⚠️ The product speaks for itself: over the recommended 10-year period, the expected gross return is 10.14%/year — but the net return after costs falls to 7.60%/year. The insurance wrapper absorbs 2.54% of your return every year.
analyseViviumSelection.s3_point1_desc: Deducted from every premium payment — identical for all Branch 23 products.
analyseViviumSelection.s3_point2_desc: The maximum distribution is 2.54% of the invested amount. This compensates the broker. It is negotiable but rarely waived — independent brokers live on these commissions.
analyseViviumSelection.s3_point3_desc: 2.23% management and administration fees + 0.02% transaction fees. On a capital of €100,000, that is €2,540/year in fees — versus €170/year for a global ETF at 0.17%. Difference: €2,370/year, every year, not compounding.
analyseViviumSelection.s3_point4_desc: Unlike other Branch 23 products reviewed on ClearInvest, Vivium requires a minimum of €25,000. This excludes the majority of retail savers and positions the product for investors with significant existing capital.
analyseViviumSelection.s4_intro: We use the product's recommended horizon (10 years) and minimum investment (€25,000), as stated in the official document.
analyseViviumSelection.s4_kid_note: Moderate scenario over 10 years, €25,000 invested: surrender value of €20,800 at maturity — a real capital loss after fees and premium tax.
analyseViviumSelection.s4_table_note: Moderate scenario over 10 years: €20,800 surrender value on €10,000 invested, an annualised net return of 7.60%. Over the same period, an ETF at 8% gross minus 0.17% fees delivers ~9.83% net. The 2.23% annual gap over 10 years represents a significant loss of compounded capital.
analyseViviumSelection.s4_methodology_note: * Methodology note: gross return of 8%/year based on the historical average of MSCI ACWI IMI (2005-2025). The official document uses 10.14% gross (moderate scenario) — we use 8% to remain consistent across all our analyses. Distribution fees are excluded from the table above (variable by broker) — including them would further worsen the result for Vivium.
analyseViviumSelection.s4_cta_text: Run the numbers with your own figures in our calculator.
analyseViviumSelection.s5_title: What about the long-term savings scheme?
analyseViviumSelection.s5_p1: Unlike AXA Index4P, Vivium Selection cannot be used as a long-term savings vehicle under normal conditions.
analyseViviumSelection.s5_p2: The long-term savings tax scheme caps deductible contributions at €2,450/year — less than 10% of Vivium's minimum entry (€25,000). The product is structurally designed for large single premiums, not for annual contributions within a tax framework.
analyseViviumSelection.s5_p3: Even if technically a first premium of €25,000 could partially qualify (only the first €2,450), the tax complexity and structural mismatch make this approach impractical.
analyseViviumSelection.s6_case1_desc: Like any Branch 23 contract, Vivium Selection allows naming specific beneficiaries who receive the capital directly upon death, outside the standard inheritance process. For investors aged 60+ looking to organise the transfer of significant capital (≥ €25,000), this feature has real value. For any other use — wealth accumulation, regular investing, tax optimisation — Vivium Selection is structurally disadvantaged compared to a direct ETF.
analyseViviumSelection.s7_verdict_p1: Vivium Selection Dynamic plus Passive is the most expensive Branch 23 product in our review: 2.54%/year RIY.
analyseViviumSelection.s7_verdict_p2: The product's irony: it invests in ETFs — the exact same instruments you could buy directly for 0.17%/year. The insurance wrapper costs you an extra 2.37%/year, every year, on your entire capital.
analyseViviumSelection.s7_verdict_p3: The €25,000 minimum excludes the majority of retail investors. And the product is not compatible with the long-term savings scheme — the only scenario where a Branch 23 product can compete with an ETF.
analyseViviumSelection.s7_ideal_desc: Wealth transfer for investors aged 60+, capital ≥ €25,000, objective of naming beneficiaries outside standard inheritance. Consult a notary before subscribing.
analyseViviumSelection.s7_avoid_desc: Any investor seeking to build wealth. The 2.54% annual fee mechanically destroys performance over any time horizon.

analyseCrelanOpportunities.meta_title: Crelan Invest Opportunities: Full Review — 2.80%/year Fees for 2.57% Net Return
analyseCrelanOpportunities.meta_description: Crelan Invest Opportunities is a fund-of-funds charging 2.80%/year delivering 2.57%/year net per its own document. Full cost analysis, ETF and XEON comparison, and verdict.
analyseCrelanOpportunities.hero_title: Crelan Invest Opportunities — 2.80%/year in fees for a 2.57% net return
analyseCrelanOpportunities.hero_subtitle: A fund-of-funds distributed by Crelan and managed by Amundi. The intermediate scenario in the official document projects 2.57%/year net — barely above a savings account, for significantly more risk.
analyseCrelanOpportunities.tldr_text: Crelan Invest Opportunities is an actively managed fund-of-funds distributed by Crelan. Its 2.80%/year total costs absorb most of the gross return: the official document shows 5.37%/year gross for 2.57%/year net — less than XEON (money-market ETF at ~2.9%/year) while carrying real market risk. The fund-of-funds structure adds a hidden double cost layer. The 1.32% securities transaction tax at exit (Belgian ISIN) makes the picture worse.
analyseCrelanOpportunities.s1_val_style: Active, fund-of-funds — 30-70% equity (ref.: 65% global equity / 35% mixed fixed income)
analyseCrelanOpportunities.s1_val_reynders: Applies to the bond portion of gains (the fund structurally holds bonds)
analyseCrelanOpportunities.s1_source: Source: official Key Information Document (KID) for this product.
analyseCrelanOpportunities.s2_title: The fund-of-funds structure: you pay twice
analyseCrelanOpportunities.s2_intro: Crelan Invest Opportunities does not invest directly in equities or bonds — it invests in other funds (fund-of-funds). This creates a double layer of costs:
analyseCrelanOpportunities.s2_layer1_desc: 2.07%/year in management fees charged by Luxcellence/Amundi for selecting and managing the portfolio of underlying funds.
analyseCrelanOpportunities.s2_layer2_desc: Each fund that Crelan Invest holds also charges its own management fees. These costs are not separately visible in the document — they are already embedded in the performance of the underlying funds before Crelan takes its own layer.
analyseCrelanOpportunities.s2_result: The total impact is 2.80%/year over the recommended holding period.
analyseCrelanOpportunities.s2_warning: ⚠️ For comparison: a World ETF (IMIE) costs 0.17%/year in a single fee layer, with no double charging. Crelan Invest Opportunities costs 2.80%/year — 16 times more expensive — for a partially similar exposure to global markets.
analyseCrelanOpportunities.s3_title: Three taxes apply to this product
analyseCrelanOpportunities.s3_point1_desc: The Belgian ISIN (BE6284594619) subjects the product to a 1.32% securities transaction tax on each sale — the highest rate, applying to Belgian-registered capitalisation SICAVs. For an ETF not registered in Belgium (such as IMIE), the rate is 0.12%.
analyseCrelanOpportunities.s3_point2_title: Reynders tax: 30% on the bond portion of gains
analyseCrelanOpportunities.s3_point2_desc: The fund can allocate between 30% and 70% to bonds depending on the manager's view. A variable fraction of your capital gain is subject to 30% withholding tax at sale — this tax does not apply to a 100% equity ETF.
analyseCrelanOpportunities.s3_point3_desc: Like all investment products since January 2026, capital gains above €10,000/year are subject to 10% CGT.
analyseCrelanOpportunities.s3_warning: ⚠️ The combination of 1.32% securities tax + Reynders tax on the bond portion of gains + 10% CGT creates a significantly heavier exit tax burden than for a World ETF (0.12% securities tax + 10% CGT only). The 1.32% securities tax alone is 11 times the securities tax on a standard ETF.
analyseCrelanOpportunities.s4_intro: Two concrete scenarios over 30 years. The Crelan return is based on the actual 5-year annualised net return (3.72%/year net of fees). The ETF return uses the 20-year historical average of the MSCI ACWI IMI (8%/year gross, or 7.83% net of fees).
analyseCrelanOpportunities.s4_callout: With monthly contributions, the ETF generates nearly 4 times more net gain. With a lump sum, the gap exceeds 4.5 times — using the fund's actual historical return, not a pessimistic assumption.
analyseCrelanOpportunities.s4_methodology: * Crelan return: 5-year annualised historical net return (source: Morningstar, data as of October 2025). ETF return: 20-year historical average of MSCI ACWI IMI (2005–2025). Note: this comparison is favorable to Crelan — IMIE's actual return over the past 5 years significantly exceeds the 8% used here, meaning the real gap is larger than shown. CGT calculated in lump-sale scenario — progressive selling reduces actual ETF taxation toward zero. The Reynders tax (30% on the fixed-income portion of capital gains) is not included in Crelan's exit tax figures — its exact calculation depends on the fund's actual bond allocation at the time of sale. Including it would increase Crelan's exit tax burden and widen the gap further, as ETF IMIE (100% equities) is entirely exempt.
analyseCrelanOpportunities.s4_cta_text: Run the numbers with your own figures in our calculator.
analyseCrelanOpportunities.s6_p1: There is no situation in which this product is justified over the available alternatives.
analyseCrelanOpportunities.s7_p1: Crelan Invest Opportunities delivered 3.72%/year net over the past 5 years — below the historical average of a simple regulated savings account over the same period, for real market risk and fees of 2.08%/year.
analyseCrelanOpportunities.s7_p2: Over 30 years at €300/month, the ETF generates nearly 4 times the net gain. On a lump sum of €50,000, the gap exceeds €300,000. This is not an opinion — it is the arithmetic of compound interest applied to actual historical returns.
analyseCrelanOpportunities.s7_ideal_desc: None for an investor with access to an online broker.
analyseCrelanOpportunities.disclaimer: Simulation for illustrative purposes only. Past performance does not guarantee future results. This content is provided for educational purposes only and does not constitute investment advice.
